topics.title: Introduction to Basic Microbiological Practices and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL307 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Basic Microbiological Practices and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Basic Microbiological Practices** refer to the standardized set of techniques, safety protocols, and procedural methodologies employed in a microbiology laboratory to prevent contamination of cultures, protect the operator from biohazards, and ensure the reliability, reproducibility, and validity of experimental results. As per **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** laboratory manuals and **Biosafety in Microbiological and Biomedical Laboratories (BMBL)** guidelines, these practices constitute the foundation of all microbiological work and are mandatory at **Biosafety Level 1 (BSL-1)** and above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCFTL307):** Module Experiment 2 emphasizes the *identification, handling, calibration, and safe operation* of core microbiological equipment along with the demonstration of fundamental aseptic practices such as flaming, streaking, and transfer of cultures.

### Definition of Key Terminology

| Term | Rigorous Academic Definition |
|------|------------------------------|
| **Aseptic Technique** | A set of procedures performed to prevent the introduction of unwanted microorganisms (contaminants) into a sterile environment, culture media, or biological sample. |
| **Sterilization** | The complete elimination or destruction of all forms of microbial life, including bacterial spores, achieved through physical or chemical means. |
| **Disinfection** | The process of eliminating pathogenic microorganisms (but not necessarily all spores) from inanimate objects using chemical agents. |
| **Decontamination** | A general term encompassing cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization to make an item safe for handling. |
| **Inoculation** | The deliberate introduction of a microorganism (or sample) into a sterile culture medium for growth and study. |
| **Sub-culturing** | The transfer of microorganisms from one culture medium to a fresh medium to maintain viability or obtain isolated colonies. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Operating Theater Model"**
>
> Imagine a **cardiac surgeon** performing open-heart surgery. Before the operation, the surgeon washes hands rigorously, wears sterile gloves, a mask, a gown, and operates inside a controlled sterile field. The instruments are autoclaved. Every surface is disinfected. Why? Because a single *Staphylococcus* from the surgeon's nose landing on the patient's heart valve could be fatal.
>
> A microbiology laboratory is essentially the **surgeon's operating theater** for invisible patients (microorganisms). The microbiologist must:
> - **Protect the culture** from contamination by ambient air, skin flora, and dust (just as the surgeon protects the patient from infection).
> - **Protect themselves** from potentially pathogenic cultures (just as the surgeon protects themselves from patient blood).
> - **Protect the environment** from releasing modified or pathogenic organisms (just as biohazard waste is incinerated).
>
> The **Bunsen burner** is the microbiologist's equivalent of the surgeon's sterile field — its convectional updraft creates a sterile working zone where airborne contaminants cannot settle easily on open Petri dishes or culture tubes.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Microbiological Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle — The Triad of Microbiological Safety**
>
> 1. **Containment of the Microbe** → Prevent escape into environment.
> 2. **Exclusion of Contaminants** → Prevent entry into sterile work.
> 3. **Protection of the Operator** → Prevent infection/contact with biohazard.

This triad is enforced through:
- **Engineering Controls:** Biological Safety Cabinets (BSC), Laminar Air Flow (LAF), autoclaves.
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Lab coat, gloves, goggles, closed shoes, masks.
- **Administrative Controls:** SOPs, training, signage, restricted access.
- **Standard Microbiological Practices (SMPs):** Hand washing, no eating/drinking, surface disinfection.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Biological Constants Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values in Food Microbiology Lab:**
>
> - **Autoclave standard sterilization cycle:** **121 °C at 15 psi (≈ 103.4 kPa) for 15 minutes**.
> - **Hot Air Oven sterilization:** **160 °C for 2 hours** or **170 °C for 1 hour**.
> - **Incubator standard temperatures:** **35 ± 2 °C** (mesophilic bacteria), **25 ± 2 °C** (yeasts/molds), **4 ± 1 °C** (psychrophilic/preservation), **55 ± 1 °C** (thermophilic).
> - **Laminar Air Flow velocity:** **0.45 m/s ± 20%** (NSF/ANSI 49 standard).
> - **BSL-1 air changes:** Minimum **6–12 air changes per hour** for lab ventilation.
> - **Standard Bunsen burner flame:** Blue cone, **flame height ≈ 5–7 cm**.
> - **Inoculating loop sterilization:** Heated to **red-hot (≈ 870 °C)** in flame.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Microbiological Equipment

The equipment used in PCFTL307 Food Microbiology Lab can be classified into **four functional categories**:

### Category A: Sterilization Equipment
| Equipment | Working Principle | Application |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Autoclave (Steam Sterilizer)** | Saturated steam under pressure (moist heat) | Media, glassware, biohazard waste |
| **Hot Air Oven** | Dry heat convection/radiation | Glassware, powders, oils (water-free items) |
| **Membrane Filtration Unit** | Physical sieving through 0.22 µm filter | Heat-sensitive liquids (sugars, antibiotics, vitamins) |
| **Bunsen Burner (Flaming)** | Direct flame exposure | Loops, needles, tube mouths |

### Category B: Culture & Incubation Equipment
| Equipment | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Bacteriological Incubator** | Maintains constant temperature for microbial growth (30 °C–60 °C range, ±0.5 °C) |
| **CO₂ Incubator** | Maintains 5% CO₂ for capnophilic organisms |
| **Shaker Incubator** | Provides agitation for aerated liquid cultures |
| **Anaerobic Jar / Chamber** | Provides N₂/H₂/CO₂ atmosphere for anaerobes |

### Category C: Observation & Measurement
| Equipment | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Compound Microscope** | Magnification up to 1000× (oil immersion) for cell morphology |
| **Stereomicroscope** | Low magnification (10–40×) for colony observation |
| **Colony Counter (Digital/Manual)** | Counts CFU/mL on agar plates |
| **Spectrophotometer** | Measures OD at 600 nm for turbidity-based growth |
| **pH Meter** | Measures medium pH (calibrated with buffers pH 4, 7, 10) |
| **Analytical Balance** | Weighing media components to **0.0001 g precision** |

### Category D: Transfer & Sample Handling
| Equipment | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Inoculating Loop & Needle** | Transfer of liquid/solid cultures |
| **Micropipettes** | Aseptic dispensing of **1 µL – 1000 µL** volumes |
| **Spreaders (L-rod / glass beads)** | Even spreading of inoculum on agar surface |
| **Vortex Mixer** | Homogenization of cell suspensions |
| **Centrifuge (Refrigerated)** | Pelleting cells; up to **15,000 × g**, 4 °C |

## 2.2 The Science of Sterilization — Why Specific Parameters?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kinetics of Microbial Inactivation**
>
> Sterilization is governed by **first-order exponential decay kinetics** for microbial inactivation. This is the foundation of all thermal sterilization calculations.

### 2.2.1 D-Value (Decimal Reduction Time)

The **D-value** is the time required at a given temperature to reduce the microbial population by **90% (1 log₁₀)**.

$$D_T = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N)}$$

Where:
- $D_T$ = D-value at temperature $T$ (minutes)
- $t$ = exposure time (minutes)
- $N_0$ = initial microbial count (CFU/mL)
- $N$ = final microbial count (CFU/mL)

### 2.2.2 Z-Value

The **Z-value** is the temperature increase (°C) required to reduce the D-value by **1 log₁₀ (i.e., to 10% of its original value)**.

$$Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2)}$$

### 2.2.3 F-Value (Sterilization Value)

The **F-value** is the equivalent time in minutes at a reference temperature (typically **121.1 °C for moist heat / 170 °C for dry heat**) that delivers the same lethal effect as the actual process.

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}}$$

Where:
- $F_0$ = sterilization value (minutes)
- $\Delta t$ = time interval between temperature readings
- $T$ = actual temperature at time $t$
- $T_{ref}$ = reference temperature (121.1 °C for steam)
- $Z$ = Z-value of reference organism (typically **Z = 10 °C** for steam sterilization, *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* spores)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Sterilization Reference Organism:** *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* (ATCC 7953) is used as the biological indicator for autoclave validation because it is a non-pathogenic spore-former with very high heat resistance ($D_{121}$ ≈ 1.5–2 min). For dry heat, *Bacillus atrophaeus* (ATCC 9372) is used.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Cheat Sheet — Microbiological Calculations**

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **D-Value** | $D_T = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N}$ | Time for 1-log kill at temp $T$ | minutes |
| **Z-Value** | $Z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10} D_1 - \log_{10} D_2}$ | Temp change for 1-log reduction in D | °C |
| **F-Value (Sterilization)** | $F_0 = \sum \Delta t \cdot 10^{\frac{T-T_{ref}}{Z}}$ | Equivalent sterilization time at $T_{ref}$ | minutes |
| **Log Reduction** | $LR = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Number of log cycles killed | dimensionless |
| **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)** | $SAL = 10^{-n}$ | Probability of non-sterile unit per unit | dimensionless |
| **CFU/mL Calculation** | $CFU/mL = \dfrac{\text{Colony Count}}{\text{Volume plated (mL)} \times \text{Dilution factor}}$ | Viable count per mL | CFU/mL |
| **Pressure-Temp Relationship (Steam)** | $T_{sat}$ from steam tables | Saturation temperature of steam at pressure | °C |
| **Total Log Reduction Required** | $\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_f)$ | For a target reduction | dimensionless |
| **Standard Autoclave Condition** | $121.1\,°C,\ 15\,psi,\ 15\,min$ | Reference moist heat sterilization | – |
| **Standard Dry Heat** | $160\,°C,\ 2\,hr$ or $170\,°C,\ 1\,hr$ | Reference dry heat sterilization | – |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students frequently confuse $D$ and $F$ values. **Remember:** $D$ = kill rate constant; $F$ = cumulative lethal effect over a process cycle.

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter — Engineering & Food Industry Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Relevance:**
>
> 1. **Canning Industry:** The $F_0$ concept directly drives the design of retort sterilization cycles for canned foods (low-acid foods require $F_0 \geq 3$ minutes for *Clostridium botulinum* control).
> 2. **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Sterile injectables must achieve **SAL = 10⁻⁶** (one non-sterile unit in one million) — calculated using $F_0$.
> 3. **Autoclave Validation:** Every autoclave must be qualified using biological indicators and thermocouple mapping; the D-value and Z-value of the indicator organism determine acceptance criteria.
> 4. **Food Microbiology Quality Control:** D and Z values are used to design pasteurization processes (e.g., HTST for milk: 72 °C, 15 s).

## 2.5 Aseptic Technique — The Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Aseptic Technique:**
>
> 1. **Sterilize the loop/needle** → flame to red-hot, allow to cool 5–10 s.
> 2. **Flame the tube mouth** → pass tube opening through flame 2× before and after transfer.
> 3. **Work near the flame** → use the **Bunsen burner updraft zone** (≤15 cm radius) as sterile field.
> 4. **Open lids minimally** → tilt Petri dish lid just enough to insert loop (the "clam-shell" or "clutch" method).
> 5. **Never blow, talk, cough, sneeze** over open cultures → always work in LAF cabinet for sensitive work.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Inactivation Model

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption
Microbial inactivation by heat follows **first-order kinetics**, analogous to radioactive decay:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of viable microorganisms at time $t$
- $k$ = inactivation rate constant (per minute)
- $t$ = time (minutes)

### 3.1.2 Separation of Variables

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot dt$$

### 3.1.3 Integration (Initial Count $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to $N$ at $t$)

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

### 3.1.4 Convert to Base 10

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot t}{2.303}$$

$$\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N) = \frac{k \cdot t}{2.303}$$

### 3.1.5 Define D-Value

By definition, when $t = D$, the count reduces by 1 log cycle, so:

$$1 = \frac{k \cdot D}{2.303} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \frac{2.303}{D}$$

### 3.1.6 Final First-Order Inactivation Equation

$$\boxed{\log_{10}(N) = \log_{10}(N_0) - \frac{t}{D}}$$

This is the **master equation** used in all sterilization validation work.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem)

### Problem Statement

> A bacterial culture has an initial count of $N_0 = 1 \times 10^{6}$ CFU/mL. The D-value at 121 °C for the target organism is **$D_{121} = 1.5$ minutes**. Calculate:
> (a) The viable count after 9 minutes of exposure at 121 °C.
> (b) The total log reduction achieved.
> (c) The time required to achieve **12-log reduction** (SAL = 10⁻¹², pharmaceutical-grade sterilization).
> (d) The F₀ value if the entire process holds at 121.1 °C reference.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
$N_0 = 1 \times 10^{6}$ CFU/mL, $D_{121} = 1.5$ min, $t = 9$ min.

**Step 2 (a):** Apply the inactivation equation.

$$\log_{10}(N) = \log_{10}(N_0) - \frac{t}{D}$$

$$\log_{10}(N) = \log_{10}(1 \times 10^{6}) - \frac{9}{1.5}$$

$$\log_{10}(N) = 6 - 6 = 0$$

$$N = 10^{0} = 1\ \text{CFU/mL}$$

**Step 3 (b):** Calculate log reduction.

$$LR = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{10^{6}}{1}\right) = 6\ \text{log reduction}$$

**Step 4 (c):** Time for 12-log reduction.

$$t_{12D} = 12 \times D = 12 \times 1.5 = 18\ \text{minutes}$$

**Step 5 (d):** Calculate $F_0$.

Since $T = T_{ref}$, $10^{\frac{T-T_{ref}}{Z}} = 10^{0} = 1$, so:

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{\frac{T-T_{ref}}{Z}} = 18 \cdot 1 = 18\ \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 12-log reduction (i.e., reducing $10^{12}$ organisms to 1) requires 18 minutes at 121 °C. The standard autoclave cycle of 15 minutes provides **10-log reduction**, which is the typical industry minimum for laboratory sterilization.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sterilization Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for Choosing Sterilization Method**

| Parameter | Autoclave (Moist Heat) | Hot Air Oven (Dry Heat) | Membrane Filtration | Radiation (UV/G) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Protein denaturation, lipid disruption | Oxidation of cellular components | Physical size exclusion | DNA damage |
| **Standard Cycle** | 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min | 160 °C, 2 hr | 0.22 µm filter, aseptic | UV: 254 nm, 30 min |
| **Spore Kill** | Excellent (high) | Excellent (highest) | Variable (depends on filter) | Limited |
| **Heat Damage** | Moderate | High | None | Low |
| **Suitable For** | Media, glassware, waste | Glassware, powders, oils | Heat-sensitive solutions | Surface sterilization (BSC) |
| **Limitations** | Cannot use for heat-labile items | Slow; burns paper | Filter clogs; not for gases | Poor penetration; no effect on spores |
| **Validation Organism** | *G. stearothermophilus* | *B. atrophaeus* | *Brevundimonas diminuta* | *B. atrophaeus* |
| **Sterility Assurance (SAL)** | 10⁻⁶ typical | 10⁻⁶ typical | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁷ | Surface only |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Autoclave Operation (Procedural Algorithm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for Autoclave Operation:**

**Step 1 — Loading:**
Place materials in autoclave-safe containers. **Never seal bottles tightly** (pressure buildup risk). Use autoclave tape (striped pattern turns black upon successful sterilization).

**Step 2 — Water Check:**
Ensure water reservoir is filled to the mark (distilled/deionized water only; tap water causes scale).

**Step 3 — Set Parameters:**
For media: 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min. For waste: 121 °C, 15 psi, **30–45 min** (to ensure penetration).

**Step 4 — Start Cycle:**
Close door securely. Verify gasket integrity. Activate cycle.

**Step 5 — Monitoring:**
Observe pressure gauge rises to 15 psi. Temperature should reach 121 °C within **8–12 minutes** of cycle start (this lag is the "come-up time", *CUT*).

**Step 6 — Sterilization Hold:**
Hold at 121 °C for 15 minutes — **THIS IS THE F₀**.

**Step 7 — Cool Down:**
Allow pressure to drop naturally (do not open vent manually). Wait until pressure gauge reads **0 psi**. Then open door slightly (1–2 cm gap) for 10 minutes to release residual steam.

**Step 8 — Unloading:**
Use heat-resistant gloves. Place media in 45 °C water bath or incubator to cool rapidly.

## 3.5 Colony Counter and CFU Calculation — Worked Example

### Problem
A food sample is serially diluted: 10⁻¹, 10⁻², 10⁻³, 10⁻⁴, 10⁻⁵. From the 10⁻⁴ dilution, **0.1 mL** is plated on Nutrient Agar using spread plate technique. After 24 hr incubation at 37 °C, the plate shows **152 colonies**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the dilution factor.
Dilution factor = $10^{4} = 10000$.

**Step 2:** Apply the CFU/mL formula.

$$CFU/mL = \frac{\text{Colonies}}{\text{Volume plated (mL)} \times \text{Dilution factor}}$$

$$CFU/mL = \frac{152}{0.1 \times 10^{4}}$$

$$CFU/mL = \frac{152}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{CFU/mL = 1.52 \times 10^{2}\ \text{CFU/mL (per the plated dilution)}}$$

**Step 3:** Correct back to original sample concentration.

$$CFU/g_{original} = 1.52 \times 10^{2} \times 10^{4} = 1.52 \times 10^{6}\ \text{CFU/mL}$$

**Step 4:** Check statistical validity.
TNTC (Too Numerous To Count) limit: **>300 colonies** → invalid count.
TFTC (Too Few To Count): **<30 colonies** → statistically unreliable.

Since 152 lies in the **30–300 acceptable range** ✓, the count is valid.

## 3.6 The 16-Item Pre-Experiment Checklist (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Evaluation Checklist — Examiner's Reference**

| # | Item | Verified? |
|---|------|-----------|
| 1 | Lab coat, gloves, closed footwear worn | ☐ |
| 2 | Hair tied back; no loose jewelry | ☐ |
| 3 | Work surface disinfected (70% ethanol) | ☐ |
| 4 | Bunsen burner lit with blue cone flame | ☐ |
| 5 | Autoclave tape color change verified | ☐ |
| 6 | Loop flamed to red-hot before & after use | ☐ |
| 7 | Tube mouth flamed before/after transfer | ☐ |
| 8 | Work performed within 15 cm of flame | ☐ |
| 9 | All cultures labeled with name, date, sample ID | ☐ |
| 10 | No mouth pipetting (mechanical pipettes only) | ☐ |
| 11 | Sharp disposables in puncture-proof container | ☐ |
| 12 | Biohazard waste in autoclave bag | ☐ |

| 14 | Hand wash at entry & exit | ☐ |
| 15 | Spills reported immediately; disinfectant applied | ☐ |
| 16 | Laboratory register entry completed | ☐ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Microbiology Laboratory Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sample Receipt\nLog Register Entry"] --> B["Pre-Processing\nWeighing & Homogenization"]
    B --> C{"Hazard\nAssessment\nBSL Decision"}
    C -->|BSL-1| D["Open Bench\nWork with PPE"]
    C -->|BSL-2| E["Laminar Air Flow\nBiosafety Cabinet"]
    C -->|BSL-3+| F["Negative Pressure\nSealed Glovebox"]
    D --> G["Inoculation Loop\nFlamed to Red Hot"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Aseptic Transfer\nTo Sterile Media"]
    H --> I["Incubation\n35°C / 24-48 hr"]
    I --> J["Observation\nColony Morphology"]
    J --> K["Identification\nGram Stain / Biochemical"]
    K --> L["Documentation\nLab Record Book"]
    H --> M["Sterilization\nAutoclave 121°C"]
    M --> N["Waste Disposal\nBiohazard Stream"]
    L --> O["Result Analysis\nCFU Calculation"]
    O --> P["Report Submission\nto Faculty"]
```

```mermaid
    end

    end

        C1["Compound Microscope"]
        C2["Colony Counter"]
        C3["Spectrophotometer 600nm"]
        C4["pH Meter"]
    end

        D1["Inoculating Loop"]
        D2["Micropipette 1-1000uL"]
        D3["L-Rod Spreader"]
        D4["Vortex Mixer"]
    end
    A --> H["Produces Sterile\nReady to Use Materials"]

    C --> F["Result &\nDocumentation"]
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Aseptic Streak Plate Procedure

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Operator
    participant F as Bunsen Burner
    participant L as Inoculating Loop
    participant P as Petri Dish with Agar
    
    O->>F: Light burner, adjust to blue cone
    O->>L: Flame loop until red hot
    O->>L: Cool loop 10 seconds in agar edge
    O->>P: Open lid slightly, streak quadrant 1
    O->>L: Reflame loop, cool
    O->>P: Streak quadrant 2 from Q1 edge
    O->>L: Reflame loop, cool
    O->>P: Streak quadrant 3 from Q2 edge
    O->>L: Reflame loop, cool
    O->>P: Streak quadrant 4 from Q3 edge
    O->>L: Reflame loop to red hot
    O->>P: Close lid, invert plate
    O->>O: Label bottom: name date sample ID
    O->>P: Incubate at 35°C for 24-48 hours
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Autoclave Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AUT["Autoclave Unit Functional Block Diagram"]
        X1["Water Reservoir\nDistilled Water"] --> X2["Boiler Chamber\nSteam Generation"]
        X2 --> X3["Steam Jacket\nSurrounds Load"]
        X3 --> X4["Sterilization Chamber\n121°C at 15 psi"]
        X4 --> X5["Thermocouple Sensors\nContinuous Monitoring"]
        X4 --> X6["Pressure Gauge\nDigital / Analog"]
        X5 --> X7["Microprocessor Controller\nCycle Validation"]
        X6 --> X7
        X7 --> X8["Safety Pressure Relief Valve\nSet at 17 psi"]
        X7 --> X9["Cycle Completion Alarm\nand Display"]
        X4 --> X10["Steam Exhaust Line\nwith Condenser"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Sterilization Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the item\nheat sensitive?"} -->|Yes| Q2{"Is it a liquid\nsolution?"}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{"Does it contain\nwater or moisture?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4["Membrane Filtration\n0.22 µm Filter"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5["Radiation / UV\nSurface Treatment"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q6["Autoclave\n121°C, 15 psi, 15 min"]
    Q3 -->|No| Q7["Hot Air Oven\n160°C, 2 hours"]
    Q4 --> Z["Validated Sterile\nReady to Use"]
    Q5 --> Z
    Q6 --> Z
    Q7 --> Z
```
