# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the IMViC tests. State the expected IMViC reaction pattern for *Escherichia coli*.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **IMViC** is a battery of four biochemical tests used to differentiate members of the *Enterobacteriaceae* family:
> - **I – Indole Test:** Detects the enzyme tryptophanase that cleaves tryptophan into indole, pyruvate, and ammonia. Positive reaction: red color with Kovac's reagent.
> - **M – Methyl Red Test:** Detects mixed-acid fermentation of glucose maintaining pH below 4.4. Positive reaction: red color.
> - **V – Voges-Proskauer Test:** Detects acetoin production from glucose fermentation. Positive reaction: pink-red color after addition of α-naphthol and KOH.
> - **C – Citrate Utilization Test:** Detects ability to use citrate as sole carbon source. Positive reaction: blue color change.
>
> **IMViC Pattern for E. coli: + + – –** (Indole positive, MR positive, VP negative, Citrate negative)

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the principle of the MUG test in E. coli identification? Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **MUG (4-Methylumbelliferyl-β-D-Glucuronide) test** is based on the enzyme **β-D-glucuronidase (GUD)** produced by >95% of *E. coli* strains. The enzyme cleaves the MUG substrate to release **4-methylumbelliferone**, which exhibits **blue fluorescence under long-wave UV light (366 nm)**.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Single-tube **definitive** test for E. coli confirmation
> - Rapid result (within 4 hours of incubation)
> - High sensitivity (>95%) and specificity (>99%)
> - Used by FSSAI and EPA as a standard confirmatory assay

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the procedure for the **Most Probable Number (MPN) method** for the enumeration of *Escherichia coli* in a food sample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water sample was analyzed by MPN technique. The following gas-positive results were obtained: 10⁻¹ → 3/3, 10⁻² → 1/3, 10⁻³ → 0/3. Calculate the MPN per 100 mL and interpret the result with respect to FSSAI drinking water standards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part A(a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 – Sample Preparation:** Homogenize 25 g (or 25 mL) of food sample in 225 mL of Buffered Peptone Water (BPW) to prepare the 10⁻¹ dilution.
>
> **Step 2 – Serial Dilution:** Prepare serial 10-fold dilutions (10⁻² through 10⁻⁶) using sterile peptone water blanks.
>
> **Step 3 – Presumptive Test:** Inoculate **three tubes of Lauryl Tryptose Broth (LTB)** per dilution with 1 mL each. Incubate at **35 ± 0.5 °C for 48 ± 3 hours**. Observe for gas production in Durham tubes and acid (yellow color).
>
> **Step 4 – Confirmatory Test:** Transfer a loopful from each positive LTB tube to **Brilliant Green Lactose Bile (BGLB) broth** with Durham tube. Incubate at **35 °C for 48 hours**. Confirm gas production.
>
> **Step 5 – Completed Test:** Streak positive BGLB tubes onto **EMB agar** and **MacConkey agar** for isolated colonies. Incubate at 35 °C for 24 hours. Observe for **metallic green sheen** on EMB and **pink lactose-fermenting** colonies on MacConkey.
>
> **Step 6 – IMViC Biochemical Confirmation:** Perform Indole, MR, VP, and Citrate tests on suspected colonies. Confirm the **+ + – –** IMViC pattern.
>
> **Step 7 – MPN Calculation:** Record the number of positive tubes per dilution. Refer to the standard **APHA MPN Table** to obtain the MPN index per 100 mL.

#### Model Answer for Part A(b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Data:**
> - Dilution 10⁻¹: 3 positive tubes
> - Dilution 10⁻²: 1 positive tube
> - Dilution 10⁻³: 0 positive tubes
> - MPN Combination Code: **3-1-0**
>
> **Step 1 — Reading MPN Index from APHA Table:**
>
> $$\text{MPN Index (3-1-0)} = 4.3 \text{ per 10 mL}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Converting to per 100 mL:**
>
> $$\text{MPN per 100 mL} = 4.3 \times 10 = 43$$
>
> **Step 3 — 95% Confidence Interval (from extended table):**
> - Lower limit: 0.7 per 10 mL → 7 per 100 mL
> - Upper limit: 9.4 per 10 mL → 94 per 100 mL
>
> **Step 4 — Interpretation:**
>
> According to **FSSAI drinking water standards (IS 10500:2012)**, the permissible limit for **fecal coliforms / E. coli** in drinking water is **0 per 100 mL** (must be absent in any 100 mL sample).
>
> Since the sample contains **43 E. coli per 100 mL**, the water is **UNFIT for drinking** and indicates **active fecal contamination**. The source requires immediate disinfection (chlorination or UV treatment) and re-sampling after remediation.

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the principle, procedure, and biochemical basis of the four **IMViC tests** for differentiating *E. coli* from *Enterobacter aerogenes*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **MUG test** and **PCR-based detection** of *E. coli* as definitive identification methods. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part B(a):

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Indole Test:**
> - **Principle:** E. coli possesses the enzyme **tryptophanase** that hydrolyzes tryptophan to indole, pyruvate, and ammonia.
> - **Procedure:** Inoculate tryptone broth, incubate 24 hours at 37 °C, add 0.5 mL Kovac's reagent.
> - **E. coli:** Positive (red ring). *E. aerogenes:* Negative.
>
> **2. Methyl Red Test:**
> - **Principle:** E. coli produces acidic end-products from glucose fermentation, lowering pH below 4.4.
> - **Procedure:** Inoculate MR-VP broth, incubate 48 hours, add methyl red indicator.
> - **E. coli:** Red (positive). *E. aerogenes:* Yellow (negative).
>
> **3. Voges-Proskauer Test:**
> - **Principle:** E. aerogenes produces acetoin (acetylmethylcarbinol) from pyruvate via α-acetolactate. Acetoin reacts with α-naphthol and creatine in alkaline conditions to form a red complex.
> - **E. coli:** Negative (no acetoin). *E. aerogenes:* Positive (red color).
>
> **4. Citrate Utilization Test:**
> - **Principle:** Organisms that can utilize citrate as sole carbon source produce alkaline byproducts, turning the bromothymol blue indicator blue.
> - **E. coli:** Negative (cannot use citrate). *E. aerogenes:* Positive (growth + blue color).
>
> **Differentiation Summary:**
>
> $$\text{IMViC Pattern: } E.\ coli = (+\,+\,-\,-) \quad \text{vs.} \quad E.\ aerogenes = (-\,-\,+\,+)$$

#### Model Answer for Part B(b):

> [!NOTE]
> **1. MUG Test:**
> - **Principle:** E. coli produces **β-D-glucuronidase (GUD)** which hydrolyzes the substrate **4-methylumbelliferyl-β-D-glucuronide (MUG)** to release 4-methylumbelliferone.
> - **Procedure:** Inoculate EC-MUG broth, incubate at 35 °C for 4 hours (rapid) or 24 hours.
> - **Observation:** Visualize under **long-wave UV light (366 nm)**. A **blue fluorescence** confirms E. coli.
> - **Significance:** Definitive, single-step confirmatory test with >95% sensitivity.
>
> **2. PCR Detection:**
> - **Principle:** Amplifies the **uidA gene** (encoding β-glucuronidase) using species-specific primers.
> - **Procedure:**
>   1. Extract genomic DNA from isolated colony or broth culture.
>   2. Set up PCR with **uidA-specific primers** (forward: 5'-AAAACGGGCGTTACCAGACT-3', reverse: 5'-ACCTGCGATGTCGGTTTTTC-3').
>   3. Thermal cycling: Initial denaturation 95 °C / 5 min → 35 cycles of (95 °C / 30 s → 58 °C / 30 s → 72 °C / 30 s) → Final extension 72 °C / 7 min.
>   4. Run amplicon on **1.5% agarose gel** stained with ethidium bromide.
> - **Expected Result:** A **147 bp amplicon** confirms E. coli.
> - **Significance:** Highly specific (100%), rapid (<24 hours total), and can detect **viable but non-culturable (VBNC)** cells.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Incorrect IMViC pattern** — Students often confuse MR+ with MR– or write VP+ for E. coli. Always remember: **E. coli = + + – –**.
> 2. **Confusing presumptive vs confirmatory tests** — The LTB test is **presumptive**; BGLB is **confirmatory**; plating + IMViC is **completed**. Mixing these stages costs marks.
> 3. **MPN formula errors** — Always multiply the MPN index from the table by the appropriate volume/dilution factor. Forgetting to convert from per 10 mL to per 100 mL is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **MUG fluorescence wavelength** — Must be stated as **366 nm (long-wave UV)**; 254 nm is incorrect.
> 5. **EC broth temperature** — Must be **44.5 ± 0.2 °C**, not 37 °C. This temperature is what distinguishes **fecal coliforms** from total coliforms.
> 6. **Forgetting controls** — In practical exams, mentioning **positive control (E. coli K-12)** and **negative control (Enterobacter aerogenes ATCC 13048)** earns additional methodology marks.
> 7. **Unit mistakes** — MPN is reported as **MPN per g (solid food)** or **MPN per 100 mL (water)**, not per mL.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **E. coli** = Gram-negative rod, lactose fermenter, fecal coliform, IMViC **+ + – –**
> - **Selective enrichment:** Lauryl Tryptose Broth (LTB) at 35 °C / 48 hr
> - **Confirmatory:** BGLB broth → gas production
> - **Completed test:** EMB agar (metallic green sheen) + MacConkey agar (pink LF colonies)
> - **IMViC reagents:** Kovac's (Indole), Methyl Red dye, α-naphthol + KOH (VP), Bromothymol blue (Citrate)
> - **EC broth** at **44.5 °C** distinguishes fecal coliforms from environmental coliforms
> - **MUG test:** β-glucuronidase → blue fluorescence at 366 nm
> - **PCR target:** **uidA gene** → **147 bp amplicon**
> - **MPN Table:** Three-tube series, dilution code 3-2-0 → index **9.3 per 100 mL**
> - **FSSAI limit:** **0 E. coli per 100 mL** of drinking water
> - **Generation time:** ~**20 minutes**; optimal temperature **37 °C**
> - **Pathogenic strain:** *E. coli* **O157:H7** — does not ferment sorbitol (use **CT-SMAC agar** for isolation)
> - **Definitive tests:** MUG fluorescence, uidA PCR, 16S rRNA sequencing
> - **Presumptive vs Definitive:** Presumptive tests require confirmation; MUG and PCR are considered definitive.
> - **Fecal coliform vs Total coliform:** Fecal coliform = growth at 44.5 °C; Total coliform = growth at 35 °C
> - **β-D-glucuronidase** is absent in *Shigella*, *Salmonella*, and most *Enterobacter* — making it highly specific for E. coli.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods for Identification of Staphylococcus aureus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL307 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Methods for Identification of Staphylococcus aureus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧫 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Staphylococcus aureus** is a **Gram-positive**, **catalase-positive**, **coagulase-positive**, non-motile, non-spore-forming coccus belonging to the family *Staphylococcaceae*. It typically arranges itself in irregular, **grape-like clusters** due to division along multiple planes. In food microbiology, *S. aureus* is recognized as one of the foremost agents of **staphylococcal food poisoning (SFP)**, primarily mediated by the ingestion of preformed **heat-stable enterotoxins** (SEA–SEE) produced in contaminated food.

**Identification** of *S. aureus* in foods follows a **polyphasic taxonomic approach** — a sequential pipeline combining:

1. **Pre-enrichment / enrichment** in selective broths
2. **Plating on selective & differential agar media** (Baird-Parker Agar, Mannitol Salt Agar)
3. **Primary biochemical screening** (Catalase, Coagulase, DNase, Hemolysis)
4. **Confirmatory biochemical profiling** (Sugar fermentations, Phosphatase, Gelatinase)
5. **Molecular / immunological confirmation** (PCR for *nuc* and *coa* genes, Latex agglutination)
6. **Automated identification systems** (VITEK 2, MALDI-TOF MS, API Staph)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective's Toolkit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine *S. aureus* as a suspect in a food-poisoning case. A detective doesn't rely on a single clue — instead, multiple lines of evidence are gathered. The **microscope** is the eyewitness (cell shape & Gram reaction), the **selective agar** is the neighborhood stake-out (only suspects flourish here), the **coagulase test** is the fingerprint (unique to *S. aureus* among staphylococci), and **PCR** is the DNA match — irrefutable proof. Each test narrows the suspect pool until only *S. aureus* remains.

## 1.3 Why Identification Matters in Food Microbiology

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Public Health** | *S. aureus* causes ~241,000 foodborne illnesses annually in the US (CDC estimate) |
| **Regulatory** | FDA & FSSAI mandate zero tolerance of coagulase-positive staphylococci in certain ready-to-eat foods |
| **Enterotoxin Risk** | Toxins are **heat-stable** (survive boiling, pasteurization, even reheating) |
| **Food Categories at Risk** | Dairy, bakery (creams, custards), processed meats, salads, fermented foods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Limit:** Regulatory microbiological limits — e.g., **10²–10⁴ CFU/g** of coagulase-positive staphylococci is the typical action/rejection threshold in processed foods as per **FSSAI (2011)** and **ICMSF** guidelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Stepwise Identification Logic — The Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> Each step eliminates non-target organisms and progressively confirms *S. aureus*. The order of tests is **critical** — running molecular assays without biochemical pre-screening wastes reagents and time.

### **Stage 1: Pre-Enrichment & Enrichment**

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Revive injured cells; increase cell density to detectable levels |
| **Media Used** | **Tryptic Soy Broth (TSB)** + **10% NaCl** (selective for halotolerant *Staphylococcus*) |
| **Incubation** | **35–37 °C** for **18–24 h** |
| **Mechanism** | Salt inhibits Gram-negative competitors while staphylococci thrive |

### **Stage 2: Selective & Differential Plating**

#### 🟫 **Baird-Parker Agar (BPA)** — *The Gold Standard*

- **Composition highlights:** Egg yolk emulsion (lecithin), **potassium tellurite (0.01%)**, lithium chloride, glycine, pyruvate.
- **Principle of Selection:**
  - *K₂TeO₃* → inhibits Gram-negative bacteria and most Gram-positive non-targets.
  - **Tellurite reduction** by *S. aureus* → intracellular deposition of metallic tellurium → **black colonies**.
  - **Lecithinase (egg-yolk reaction)** → cleaves lecithin → clear zones (halos) around colonies.
  - **Lipolytic activity** → opaque zones (clearing).
- **Typical colony:** **2–5 mm diameter, shiny black, convex, surrounded by clear halo, with or without opaque ring**.
- **Important note:** Coagulase-negative staphylococci (CoNS) and some *S. epidermidis* may also form small black colonies — hence, confirmatory tests are mandatory.

#### 🟨 **Mannitol Salt Agar (MSA)**

- Contains **7.5% NaCl** (selective) and **mannitol** + **phenol red indicator** (differential).
- *S. aureus* ferments mannitol → acidic byproducts → **pH drops** → **phenol red turns yellow**.
- **CoNS** do not ferment mannitol → medium remains **red/pink**.
- **Limitation:** Some *S. saprophyticus* and *S. haemolyticus* also ferment mannitol; further confirmation is needed.

### **Stage 3: Primary Biochemical Tests**

#### 2.3.1 Gram Stain & Cell Morphology
- **Result for *S. aureus*:** **Gram-positive cocci, 0.5–1.5 µm diameter, in irregular grape-like clusters**.

#### 2.3.2 Catalase Test
- **Reagent:** **3% H₂O₂**
- **Principle:** Catalase decomposes H₂O₂ → H₂O + O₂ (bubbles).
- **Result:** **Positive** (vigorous bubbling within 5–10 seconds).
- **Purpose:** Differentiates *Staphylococcus* (catalase **+ve**) from *Streptococcus* (catalase **−ve**).

#### 2.3.3 Coagulase Test (The Hallmark of *S. aureus*)
- **Two forms:**
  - **Bound Coagulase (Clumping Factor):** Bound to cell wall → detected by **slide test**.
  - **Free Coagulase:** Secreted → converts fibrinogen → fibrin → detected by **tube test**.
- **Reagent:** Rabbit **EDTA-plasma** (with or without brain-heart infusion).
- **Tube Test Procedure:** Mix 0.5 mL plasma + 0.1 mL overnight culture → incubate at **37 °C** → observe at 1, 3, 6, 24 h.
- **Result Interpretation:**
  - **Tube coagulase positive (TC+):** Clot formation = *S. aureus* confirmed.
  - **TC-negative but slide-positive** → atypical strains; needs **late clumping factor / protein A profiling**.

#### 2.3.4 DNase Test
- **Medium:** **DNase agar (Toluidine Blue O)**
- **Principle:** *S. aureus* secretes **thermonuclease (TNase)** → hydrolyzes DNA → clear zone around colony.
- **Toluidine blue** complexes with DNA → metachromatic blue; digested areas appear **clear pink**.
- **Result:** **Clear halo** = **DNase positive**.
- **Importance:** TNase is a stable indicator — correlates with **enterotoxin-producing strains**.

#### 2.3.5 Hemolysis on Blood Agar
- **Medium:** **5% sheep blood agar (tryptic soy base)**
- **Types of hemolysis observed for *S. aureus*:**
  - **β-hemolysis** (complete) — most common; **clear zone** around colony.
  - **α-hemolysis** (partial, greenish) — uncommon.
  - **γ-hemolysis** (no hemolysis) — rare in clinical/food isolates.

### **Stage 4: Confirmatory & Supplementary Tests**

| Test | Target | Result for *S. aureus* |
|---|---|---|
| **Mannitol fermentation (MSA)** | Mannitol → acid | **Positive** (yellow) |
| **Phosphatase test** | Phenolphthalein phosphate → pink | **Positive** |
| **Gelatinase** | Gelatin liquefaction | **Positive** |
| **Urease** | Urea hydrolysis | **Variable / weakly positive** |
| **Nitrate reduction** | NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ | **Positive** |
| **Methyl Red / Voges-Proskauer** | Glucose metabolism | **MR + / VP +** |
| **Novobiocin sensitivity** | Differential | **Sensitive** (vs. *S. saprophyticus* = resistant) |
| **Polymyxin B resistance** | Differential | **Resistant** |
| **Bacitracin (0.04 U) sensitivity** | Differential | **Sensitive** |

### **Stage 5: Molecular & Immunological Methods**

#### 2.5.1 PCR-based Detection
- **Target genes:**
  - ***nuc* gene** (thermonuclease) — **species-specific**, gold-standard marker.
  - ***coa* gene** (coagulase) — distinguishes *S. aureus* from CoNS.
  - ***spa* gene** (protein A) — typing and identification.
  - ***mecA* gene** — methicillin resistance (MRSA) — important in food chain surveillance.
  - **Enterotoxin genes:** *sea, seb, sec, sed, see* — for enterotoxigenic strains.
- **Procedure:** DNA extraction → PCR amplification → agarose gel electrophoresis → amplicon size confirmation.

#### 2.5.2 Latex Agglutination Test
- **Principle:** Latex beads coated with antibodies against *S. aureus* clumping factor, protein A, and capsular polysaccharides.
- **Result:** **Agglutination within 30 seconds** = positive.

#### 2.5.3 ELISA / SET-RPLA
- **Reverse Passive Latex Agglutination (RPLA)** kits (e.g., Oxoid SET-RPLA) detect **enterotoxins** (A–E) in food filtrates.

#### 2.5.4 Automated Systems
- **VITEK 2** — biochemical card-based identification (ID-GP, GP-ID, AST-GP cards).
- **MALDI-TOF MS** — protein mass fingerprinting; matches spectrum to database; rapid (<1 min/isolate).
- **API Staph** — miniaturized biochemical gallery.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ Markdown Pipe Rule:** Vertical pipes inside mathematical expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the literal `|` character to preserve table syntax integrity.

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell size (diameter)** | $d$ | $0.5 \leq d \leq 1.5$ | $\mu m$ |
| **Coagulase reaction time** | $t_{clot}$ | $1 \leq t_{clot} \leq 24$ | h |
| **BPA incubation** | $T_{BPA}$ | $37$ | °C |
| **BPA incubation duration** | $t_{inc}$ | $45 \leq t_{inc} \leq 48$ | h |
| **MSA NaCl conc.** | $C_{NaCl}$ | $7.5$ | $\%$ |
| **H₂O₂ conc. (Catalase test)** | $C_{H_2O_2}$ | $3$ | $\% \ (v/v)$ |
| **Blood conc. in Blood Agar** | $C_{blood}$ | $5$ | $\% \ (v/v)$ |
| **Tellurite conc. (BPA)** | $C_{TeO_3}$ | $0.01$ | $\% \ (w/v)$ |
| **Salt conc. in Sel. Broth** | $C_{salt}$ | $10$ | $\% \ (w/v)$ |
| **Log CFU threshold (regulatory)** | $\log_{10}(N_{threshold})$ | $2 \leq \log_{10}(N) \leq 4$ | $\log \ CFU/g$ |
| **Colony Count Calculation** | $N = \dfrac{\sum C}{V \cdot d \cdot n}$ | — | $CFU/mL$ |
| **Molecular weight of Nuc enzyme** | $M_{Nuc}$ | $\approx 16.8$ | $kDa$ |
| **Staph enterotoxin thermal stability** | $T_{toxin,stable}$ | $100$ (boiling, 30 min) | °C |

Where:
- $N$ = estimated CFU count
- $C$ = colonies counted on plates
- $V$ = volume plated (mL)
- $d$ = dilution factor
- $n$ = number of replicates

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility

- **Dairy industry:** Routine QC of pasteurized milk, cheeses, and dairy desserts.
- **Meat processing plants:** Swab-based detection on cutting surfaces, processed meat.
- **Bakery & confectionery:** Monitoring cream-filled pastries, custards, and egg-based products.
- **Canned food / RTE foods:** Regulatory compliance testing.
- **HACCP critical control points:** *S. aureus* counts define CCP verification.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Decision Logic

## 3.1 Standard Bacteriological Workflow — Detailed Protocol

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step protocol** that students are expected to record in their lab manuals. Every action, every reagent quantity, and every incubation condition is explicitly stated.

### **Step 1: Sample Preparation**

1. Weigh **25 g** of food sample aseptically into a sterile stomacher bag.
2. Add **225 mL of Buffered Peptone Water (BPW)** — this gives the **1:10 initial dilution** ($10^{-1}$).
3. Homogenize in stomacher for **30–60 seconds** at normal speed.
4. Prepare serial 10-fold dilutions: $10^{-2}, 10^{-3}, 10^{-4}$ in **9 mL BPW blanks** each.

$$
\text{Dilution factor at tube } n = 10^{-n}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$

### **Step 2: Enrichment (Optional for Low-Count Foods)**

5. Inoculate **1 mL** of $10^{-1}$ dilution into **9 mL TSB + 10% NaCl**.
6. Incubate at **37 °C** for **18–24 h** to resuscitate stressed cells.

### **Step 3: Selective Plating**

7. Spread-plate **0.1 mL** from each dilution onto:
   - **Baird-Parker Agar (BPA)** — duplicate plates.
   - **Mannitol Salt Agar (MSA)** — duplicate plates.
8. Incubate BPA at **37 °C for 45–48 h** (observe at 24 h and 48 h).
9. Incubate MSA at **37 °C for 24–48 h**.

### **Step 4: Colony Selection & Purification**

10. From BPA, select **5–10 typical colonies** (black, shiny, 2–5 mm, surrounded by clear halos).
11. Streak-purify on **Nutrient Agar (NA)** slants / plates.
12. From MSA, select yellow, mannitol-fermenting colonies.
13. Maintain purified cultures at **4 °C** for downstream testing.

### **Step 5: Gram Stain**

14. Prepare smear on clean glass slide → heat-fix.
15. Stain sequence: **Crystal violet (60 s) → Gram's iodine (60 s) → Decolourizer (alcohol, 10–20 s) → Safranin (30 s)**.
16. Blot dry → observe under oil immersion (100×).
17. **Expected result:** **Gram-positive cocci in grape-like clusters**.

### **Step 6: Catalase Test**

18. Place a loopful of culture on a clean glass slide.
19. Add **1 drop of 3% H₂O₂**.
20. **Positive reaction:** **Immediate bubbling (effervescence of O₂ gas)**.
21. *S. aureus* gives **catalase positive** result.

$$
\text{Catalase reaction:} \quad 2H_2O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalase}} 2H_2O + O_2 \uparrow
$$

### **Step 7: Coagulase Test (Tube Method — Confirmatory)**

22. Add **0.5 mL** of reconstituted rabbit plasma to a sterile tube.
23. Add **0.1 mL** of overnight TSB culture.
24. Mix gently; incubate at **37 °C** in a water bath.
25. Read at **1 h, 3 h, 6 h, and 24 h**.
26. **Positive result:** Visible clot formation; tilting tube does not dislodge contents.
27. **Negative result:** No clot; remains liquid.
28. **Quality control:** *S. aureus* ATCC 25923 = positive; *S. epidermidis* ATCC 14990 = negative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** The tube coagulase test is the **gold standard** for confirmatory identification. A single slide test result is **not acceptable** as a standalone confirmatory test — must always be backed by the tube test.

### **Step 8: DNase Test**

29. Spot-inoculate DNase agar plate (Toluidine Blue O) with suspected colonies.
30. Incubate at **37 °C for 18–24 h**.
31. **Positive result:** **Clear pink zone** surrounding the colony (DNA hydrolysis).
32. Flood plate with **1 N HCl** — undigested DNA precipitates as cloudy blue; digested regions remain clear (alternative reading).

### **Step 9: Hemolysis Test**

33. Streak pure culture on **5% sheep blood agar**.
34. Incubate at **37 °C for 24 h**.
35. **Positive for β-hemolysis:** **Clear, colorless zone** surrounding colony.
36. Confirm by holding plate against light.

### **Step 10: Novobiocin Sensitivity (Differential from *S. saprophyticus*)**

37. Place **Novobiocin 5 µg disc** on inoculated Mueller-Hinton agar.
38. Incubate at **37 °C for 16–18 h**.
39. **Result for *S. aureus*:** **Sensitive (≥16 mm zone of inhibition)**.
40. *S. saprophyticus* = **Resistant**.

### **Step 11: Molecular Confirmation (PCR — for nuc gene)**

41. Extract genomic DNA using standard kit (lysozyme + proteinase K + silica column).
42. Set up PCR master mix:

$$
\text{Master Mix (50 \mu L)}: \text{DNA template (1 \mu L)} + 10X \text{ buffer (5 \mu L)} + dNTPs (200 \mu M) + MgCl_2 (1.5 mM) + \text{Nuc-F primer (1 \mu M)} + \text{Nuc-R primer (1 \mu M)} + \text{Taq polymerase (1 U)} + \text{nuclease-free water (q.s.)}
$$

43. Thermal cycling conditions:

$$
\text{Initial denaturation:} \quad 95^\circ C, \ 5 \ min
$$

$$
\text{Denaturation:} \quad 95^\circ C, \ 30 \ s
$$

$$
\text{Annealing:} \quad 55^\circ C, \ 30 \ s
$$

$$
\text{Extension:} \quad 72^\circ C, \ 45 \ s \ \times \ 30 \ \text{cycles}
$$

$$
\text{Final extension:} \quad 72^\circ C, \ 7 \ min
$$

44. Load **5 µL PCR product + 1 µL loading dye** on **1.5% agarose gel**.
45. Run at **100 V for 30–45 min**; visualize under UV transilluminator.
46. **Expected amplicon size:** **279 bp** for the *nuc* gene.

### **Step 12: CFU Counting & Enumeration**

47. Select plates with **30 ≤ C ≤ 300 colonies** for reliable count.
48. Compute CFU per gram using:

$$
N = \dfrac{\sum C}{V \cdot d \cdot n}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = microbial count per gram of food ($CFU/g$)
- $\sum C$ = sum of colonies on selected plates
- $V$ = volume plated in mL
- $d$ = dilution factor (e.g., $10^{-3}$)
- $n$ = number of plates

**Worked Example:**
If a plate at $10^{-3}$ dilution has **120 colonies** and 0.1 mL was plated:

$$
N = \dfrac{120}{0.1 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1} = \dfrac{120}{10^{-4}} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \ CFU/g
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logistic Interpretation:** This value $1.2 \times 10^{6} \ CFU/g$ **exceeds the FSSAI limit of $10^{4} \ CFU/g$** — the food sample is **non-compliant** and rejected for human consumption.

## 3.2 Decision Logic Matrix — "Is It *S. aureus*?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this decision matrix to **interpret a complete biochemical profile** after all tests are done.

| Test Performed | *S. aureus* (Expected) | If Result Differs |
|---|---|---|
| **Gram stain** | Gram +ve cocci, clusters | → Repeat staining / check purity |
| **Catalase** | **Positive** | If −ve → Not *Staphylococcus* |
| **Coagulase (tube)** | **Positive** | If −ve → *S. epidermidis* or CoNS |
| **DNase** | **Positive** | Correlates with enterotoxigenicity |
| **Mannitol fermentation** | **Positive** (yellow) | CoNS = negative (red) |
| **β-hemolysis** | **Positive** | Some MRSA non-hemolytic |
| **Novobiocin** | **Sensitive** | *S. saprophyticus* = resistant |
| **BPA colony** | Black, halo | Recheck media quality |
| **PCR for *nuc*** | **279 bp amplicon** | Final confirmatory proof |

**Identification rule:** A strain is confirmed as ***Staphylococcus aureus*** **only when all of the following are positive:**
**Gram +ve cocci in clusters + Catalase +ve + Coagulase (tube) +ve + DNase +ve + β-hemolytic + nuc PCR +ve**.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Q1]**
*List the selective and differential agents in Baird-Parker Agar. How does each contribute to the identification of* Staphylococcus aureus*?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Agent | Concentration | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Potassium tellurite** | 0.01 % | Selective — inhibits Gram-negative organisms; reduced by *S. aureus* to metallic tellurium → **black colonies** |
| **Lithium chloride** | 0.4 % | Selective — inhibits most other Gram-positive bacteria |
| **Glycine** | 1.2 % | Selective — inhibits many non-target bacteria |
| **Egg yolk emulsion (lecithin)** | 5 % | Differential — *S. aureus* produces **lecithinase** → clears egg yolk → **clear halo** around colony |
| **Pyruvate** | 1 % | Restorative — promotes growth of stressed *S. aureus* cells |
| **Pancreatic digest of casein / yeast extract** | — | Nutritional base |

**(1 mark** for listing 4 agents with concentration; **1 mark** for explaining tellurite mechanism; **1 mark** for explaining lecithinase mechanism**)

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Q2]**
*Distinguish between the **slide coagulase** and **tube coagulase** tests. Which is confirmatory for* S. aureus *and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Slide Coagulase | Tube Coagulase |
|---|---|---|
| **Coagulase type** | Bound (clumping factor, ClfA) | Free (secreted coagulase) |
| **Reagent** | Rabbit plasma on a slide | Rabbit plasma in a tube (0.5 mL) |
| **Time to result** | 10–30 seconds | 1–24 h |
| **Reaction** | Clumping of cells | Visible clot formation |
| **Purpose** | Screening | **Confirmatory** (gold standard) |
| **Specificity** | Lower (false positives) | Higher — definitive for *S. aureus* |

The **tube coagulase test** is the **confirmatory test** because it detects the actual enzymatic conversion of fibrinogen → fibrin by the secreted coagulase enzyme — a defining virulence trait of *S. aureus*. The slide test has false positives due to non-specific clumping. (**3 marks — 1 for differentiation, 1 for explaining the enzyme, 1 for justifying the gold standard**)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 / Module 2]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Describe in detail the cultural characteristics of* Staphylococcus aureus *on Baird-Parker Agar and Mannitol Salt Agar. How are these media made selective and differential?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Baird-Parker Agar (BPA) — Cultural Characteristics:**
1. **Colony morphology:** Black, shiny, convex, circular, 2–5 mm diameter (**0.5 mark**)
2. **Surrounded by clear halo** due to lecithinase activity on egg yolk (**0.5 mark**)
3. **Opaque zone** (clearing of lipovitellin / lipolysis) may be present (**0.5 mark**)
4. After 24 h: small, pinpoint black colonies; after 48 h: mature colonies with full halo (**0.5 mark**)

**Selective agents:** K₂TeO₃ (0.01 %), LiCl (0.4 %), glycine (**0.5 mark**)
**Differential agents:** Egg yolk emulsion (lecithinase), pyruvate for reactivation (**0.5 mark**)

**Mannitol Salt Agar (MSA) — Cultural Characteristics:**
1. **Mannitol fermenters (S. aureus):** **Bright yellow colonies** on a yellow medium background — acidic fermentation of mannitol (**0.5 mark**)
2. **Non-mannitol fermenters (CoNS):** Small red/pink colonies on unchanged red medium (**0.5 mark**)
3. **Selective agent:** 7.5 % NaCl — inhibits most bacteria except halotolerant *Staphylococcus* (**0.5 mark**)
4. **Differential system:** Mannitol + phenol red indicator; fermentation → acid → pH drop → yellow color (**0.5 mark**)
5. **Limitation:** Some *S. saprophyticus* and *S. haemolyticus* also ferment mannitol → false positives (**0.5 mark**)
6. **Comparison summary table** comparing BPA vs MSA selectivity, differential principle, and colony appearance (**1 mark**)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the principle, procedure, and interpretation of the **coagulase tube test**. Why is it considered the gold standard for* S. aureus *identification?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
- *S. aureus* secretes **free coagulase (Coa)** which binds to host **prothrombin**, forming **staphylothrombin** — a complex that converts **fibrinogen → fibrin**, resulting in a visible **clot** in plasma.
- The reaction is detected in vitro using **rabbit plasma** (preferred over human, as it is standardized and free of antibodies).

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Reconstitute lyophilized rabbit EDTA-plasma with sterile distilled water.
2. Dispense **0.5 mL** of plasma into a small sterile test tube.
3. Add **0.1 mL** of an 18–24 h old TSB culture of the test organism.
4. Include **positive control** (*S. aureus* ATCC 25923) and **negative control** (*S. epidermidis* ATCC 14990).
5. Incubate at **37 °C in a water bath**.
6. Read at **1 h, 3 h, 6 h, and 24 h** by gently tilting the tube.
7. A positive reaction may show any degree of clot — from slight to complete solidification.

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Positive:** Any visible clot (gelling or solidification) within 24 h → ***S. aureus*** confirmed.
- **Negative:** No clot; plasma remains liquid.
- **Clumping factor-positive but tube-negative** strains → atypical; requires further testing (e.g., DNase, PCR).
- **False negatives:** Aged cultures, excessive inoculum, oxalate plasma (use EDTA or citrate plasma only).

**Why Gold Standard? (1 mark):**
- Coagulase production is the **defining phenotypic trait** that distinguishes *S. aureus* from all other staphylococci.
- Has high **specificity (≈ 99 %)** and correlates with **virulence** (clotting protects the bacterium from host immune defenses).
- Recognized by **BAM, FDA, ISO 6888-1, ICMSF** as a confirmatory test for *S. aureus* in foods.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 / Module 2 - Alt]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Describe the molecular methods used for the identification and typing of* Staphylococcus aureus *in food samples. Include the major target genes and their significance.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. PCR-based Detection (3 marks):**

| Target Gene | Function | Amplicon Size | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| ***nuc*** | Thermonuclease | 279 bp | **Species-specific** — gold-standard molecular marker |
| ***coa*** | Coagulase | Variable (500–1000 bp) | Confirms coagulase production at gene level |
| ***spa*** | Protein A | Variable | Typing — useful for epidemiological studies |
| ***mecA*** | PBP2a (methicillin resistance) | 533 bp | **MRSA detection** — critical in food chain surveillance |
| ***sea–see*** | Enterotoxins A–E | Variable | **Enterotoxigenic strain** identification — direct food-poisoning risk |

**PCR workflow:** DNA extraction (silica column / boiling) → Master mix preparation → Thermal cycling (35 cycles) → Agarose gel electrophoresis → Amplicon detection under UV (ethidium bromide or SYBR Green).

**2. Real-Time PCR (qPCR) (1.5 marks):**
- Faster, quantitative, higher sensitivity.
- Uses SYBR Green or TaqMan probes.
- Can detect <10 CFU/g of food after enrichment.

**3. Multiplex PCR (1.5 marks):**
- Simultaneous detection of multiple targets (*nuc* + *mecA* + *sea*).
- Saves time and reagents in surveillance studies.

**4. 16S rRNA Gene Sequencing (1 mark):**
- Universal bacterial identification; *S. aureus* 16S rRNA gene is **~1,550 bp**.
- Useful for phylogenetic confirmation, but less specific than *nuc* PCR for *S. aureus*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Discuss the role of enterotoxins in staphylococcal food poisoning. Explain the methods used for enterotoxin detection in food.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Enterotoxins — Overview (2 marks):**
- *S. aureus* produces **Staphylococcal Enterotoxins (SEs)** — **SEA, SEB, SEC, SED, SEE** are the classical five; newer types (SEG–SEU) are recognized.
- **Molecular weight:** **24–28 kDa**; **single-chain globular proteins**.
- **Heat-stable:** Resist **100 °C for 30 min**, gastric proteases, and freezing.
- **Mechanism of action:** Superantigens → bind MHC-II + TCR outside the antigen groove → polyclonal T-cell activation → massive cytokine release → **emesis and gastroenteritis**.
- **Symptoms:** Nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, diarrhea; onset within **1–6 h** of ingestion; self-limiting in 24–48 h.

**Methods of Enterotoxin Detection (5 marks):**

| Method | Principle | Sensitivity / LOD | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SET-RPLA (Reverse Passive Latex Agglutination)** | Anti-SE antibodies coated on latex beads; agglutination with toxin | 0.5–1 ng/mL | 24 h |
| **ELISA (Sandwich)** | Capture + detection antibodies bound to toxin; enzymatic color change | 0.1–0.5 ng/g | 4–6 h |
| **TCA precipitation + immunoassay** | Concentrate toxin from food extract | 0.1 ng/g | 24 h |
| **PCR for enterotoxin genes** | Detects *sea–see* genes; indicates **potential** but not actual toxin | Gene level | 4–6 h |
| **Western blot** | SDS-PAGE + immunoblotting | <1 ng | 24 h |
| **Animal bioassay (Kitten / monkey emesis test)** | Historical; live-animal feeding; observe emesis | Qualitative | 6 h |
| **Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS/MS)** | Direct protein detection; quantitative | pg/mL range | 1–2 h |

**Sample preparation for food:** Homogenize 25 g food in 100 mL saline → adjust pH to 7.0 → TCA precipitation or dialysis concentration → apply to immunoassay or bioassay.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing BPA tellurite with MSA NaCl:** Many students mix up the selective principles. **BPA → tellurite** is selective; **MSA → NaCl (7.5 %)** is selective. **Egg yolk** is differential in BPA; **mannitol + phenol red** is differential in MSA. (Common -1 to -2 marks error)
>
> 2. **❌ Reporting slide coagulase as confirmatory:** The slide test is **only a screening** test. The **tube test is confirmatory**. Reporting slide as definitive = loss of 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **❌ Wrong amplicon size for *nuc* gene:** Students write 380 bp, 500 bp, 220 bp — the correct size is **279 bp**. Mistake = -1 mark.
>
> 4. **❌ Omitting quality control strains:** Failing to mention **ATCC 25923 (positive)** and **ATCC 14990 (negative)** as controls for the coagulase test costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **❌ Forgetting heat-stability of enterotoxin:** The examiner often tests this. Always emphasize: **SEs survive boiling, pasteurization, and reheating** — destruction requires **121 °C / 15 min / 15 psi autoclaving** (rarely achieved in domestic cooking).
>
> 6. **❌ Confusing β-hemolysis with α-hemolysis:** *S. aureus* = **β (clear)**, not α (green). Students often write "α-hemolysis" — automatic -1 mark.
>
> 7. **❌ Wrong incubation time for BPA:** It is **45–48 h** (not 24 h). At 24 h, colonies are immature and tellurite reduction is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Yield Points:**

- **🧫 Cell morphology:** Gram +ve cocci, **0.5–1.5 µm**, **grape-like clusters**, non-motile, non-spore-forming.
- **🟫 BPA colony:** **Black, shiny, convex, 2–5 mm**, **clear halo** (lecithinase) ± **opaque ring** (lipolysis).
- **🟨 MSA colony:** **Yellow** (mannitol +ve) on **7.5 % NaCl** medium; **red** (non-fermenters).
- **🧪 Catalase:** Positive → distinguishes *Staphylococcus* from *Streptococcus*.
- **🩸 Coagulase (tube):** **Gold standard** confirmatory; free coagulase → fibrinogen → fibrin clot.
- **🔬 DNase test:** **Toluidine Blue O** agar; **clear pink zone** = positive; correlates with **thermonuclease gene (*nuc*)**.
- **🩸 Hemolysis:** *S. aureus* is **β-hemolytic** on **5 % sheep blood agar** (clear zone, not green).
- **💊 Novobiocin:** *S. aureus* = **Sensitive**; *S. saprophyticus* = Resistant.
- **🧬 PCR confirmatory gene:** ***nuc*** → **279 bp amplicon**.
- **🦠 Enterotoxins (SEA–SEE):** **Heat-stable** (resist 100 °C, 30 min); **superantigens**; onset **1–6 h**; **emesis + diarrhea**.
- **🔢 Regulatory limit:** FSSAI / ICMSF: **10²–10⁴ CFU/g** for coagulase-positive staphylococci in foods.
- **🧂 Selective pressure:** BPA — **tellurite (0.01 %)**; MSA — **NaCl (7.5 %)**; TSB enrichment — **NaCl (10 %)**.
- **🦠 Differentials from CoNS:** Coagulase +ve + DNase +ve + β-hemolytic + Novobiocin sensitive + Mannitol +ve.
- **💉 Vaccines / Phage typing:** Optional advanced — typing methods for outbreak tracking.
- **🧊 Sample storage:** If delay > 1 h, refrigerate at **≤ 4 °C** (do not freeze — kills cells).
- **📐 CFU formula:** $N = \dfrac{\sum C}{V \cdot d \cdot n}$ — memorize for enumeration questions.
- **🐰 Plasma for coagulase:** Always **rabbit EDTA-plasma** (or citrate, not oxalate/heparin).
- **🌡️ Incubation:** All primary tests = **37 °C / 18–24 h** (except BPA = 48 h).
- **🔬 Confirmatory rule:** A strain is *S. aureus* only if **all** of: Gram+ve clusters, Catalase+ve, Tube Coagulase+ve, DNase+ve, β-hemolytic, *nuc* PCR+ve.
