topics.title: Counting for Yeasts and Moulds
topics.content:
# 🧫 Counting for Yeasts and Moulds — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTL307 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Counting for Yeasts and Moulds --- ## 📘 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Yeast and Mould Counting** is a quantitative microbiological enumeration technique used to determine the number of viable fungal propagules (vegetative cells, spores, and hyphal fragments) present per unit mass or volume of a food sample. The standard reference method employs the **Plate Count Technique** on acidified or antibiotic-supplemented media to suppress bacterial overgrowth, with results expressed as **Colony Forming Units per gram (CFU/g)** or **per millilitre (CFU/mL)** of the food matrix. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** > *"Enumeration of yeasts and moulds by pour plate and spread plate techniques using selective media like Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA) acidified with tartaric acid, incubated at 25 ± 1 °C for 3–5 days."* ### Key Terminology Decoded | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **CFU** | Colony Forming Unit — a single viable cell or clump capable of multiplying into one visible colony | | **Acidified PDA** | Potato Dextrose Agar with pH lowered to **3.5 ± 0.1** using **10% tartaric acid** to inhibit bacteria | | **Propagule** | Any reproductive unit (spore, conidium, budding cell) that can germinate into a new colony | | **Plate Count** | Standard plate count method following serial 10-fold dilutions | | **Serial Dilution** | Successive 1:10 dilutions to bring microbial load into countable range (**30–300 CFU/plate**) | --- ## 💡 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowd Counting Problem" Imagine you are at a packed stadium holding **10 lakh people** (1,000,000 spectators) and you are asked: *"How many people are in this stadium?"* Obviously, you cannot count them one by one. So, you: 1. Take a tiny representative sample (say, 1 mL from a 1,000 mL flask). 2. Dilute it 10 times, then 100 times, then 1000 times... 3. Spread a small drop on a tray and let each person "settle" as one visible dot. 4. Count the dots in the tray and multiply back by the dilution factor. **Microbial counting works exactly the same way!** We cannot see individual yeasts and moulds directly, so we **dilute** the sample, **culture** them on agar, let each viable cell form a **visible colony**, and then **mathematically back-calculate** the original population. > [!NOTE] > **Why not just count under a microscope?** Direct microscopy cannot distinguish *viable* cells from *dead* cells. Only culture-based methods confirm metabolic activity and reproductive capability. --- ## 🎯 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Standards) - **Standard Incubation Temperature:** **25 ± 1 °C** (favourable for mesophilic fungi; lower than bacterial 37 °C incubation) - **Standard Incubation Time:** **3 to 5 days** (fungi grow slower than bacteria) - **Optimal Counting Range per Plate:** **30 to 300 CFU** (statistically reliable) - **Medium pH (Acidified PDA):** **3.5 ± 0.1** - **Tartaric Acid Concentration:** ~10% solution added post-autoclaving - **Standard Dilution Factor:** **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁷** for most foods - **Standard Inoculum Volume:** **0.1 mL (spread plate)** or **1.0 mL (pour plate)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state units as **CFU/g for solids** and **CFU/mL for liquids**. Reporting "colonies per plate" without back-calculation is a guaranteed mark deduction. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 📐 2.1 The Two Principal Enumeration Methods ### 🥇 Method A — Pour Plate Technique The pour plate method is the **gold standard reference method** for yeast and mould counting in KTU laboratory examinations. #### Operational Steps 1. **Sample Preparation:** Homogenize 25 g (or 25 mL) of food sample in 225 mL of sterile **0.1% peptone water** (or sterile distilled water). This is the **10⁻¹ dilution**. 2. **Serial Dilution:** Transfer 1 mL from 10⁻¹ into 9 mL diluent → **10⁻²**. Repeat until **10⁻⁶ or 10⁻⁷**. 3. **Inoculation:** Pipette **1.0 mL** from the chosen dilution into an **empty, sterile Petri dish**. 4. **Pouring:** Add **15–20 mL** of cooled, molten (45–50 °C) acidified PDA. 5. **Mixing:** Swirl gently in an **8-shaped pattern** to disperse. 6. **Solidification:** Let the agar set on a flat surface. 7. **Incubation:** Invert plates and incubate at **25 ± 1 °C for 3–5 days**. #### ✅ Advantages - Quantitative and reproducible - Allows anaerobic/semi-anaerobic growth (moulds can grow subsurface) - Standard IS 5403 / APHA method #### ❌ Disadvantages - Heat shock: Molten agar (45–50 °C) may injure heat-sensitive mould spores - Surface colonies can be obscured by subsurface growth - Colonies may be embedded in agar → difficult to count --- ### 🥈 Method B — Spread Plate Technique #### Operational Steps 1. Prepare serial dilutions as above. 2. Pour **15–20 mL** of acidified PDA into a Petri dish and **let it solidify** first. 3. Pipette **0.1 mL** of the chosen dilution onto the centre of the solidified agar. 4. Spread uniformly using a **sterile glass L-rod (Drigalski spatula)** or disposable spreader. 5. Allow the inoculum to **absorb completely** (no visible liquid). 6. Incubate inverted at **25 ± 1 °C for 3–5 days**. #### ✅ Advantages - All colonies grow on the **surface** → easy to count and identify - No heat shock — ideal for sensitive moulds and yeast - Allows morphological identification of yeasts vs. moulds #### ❌ Disadvantages - Only **0.1 mL** can be spread → lower sample volume - Requires pre-poured plates (extra preparation time) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** When asked "which method is preferred for yeasts?", answer **Spread Plate** (no heat injury). For "official standard method", answer **Pour Plate** (APHA/IS reference). --- ## 🧪 2.2 Selective Media — The Chemistry Behind Selectivity ### 🥔 Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA) — Composition | Component | Amount (per litre) | Function | |-----------|--------------------|----------| | Potato infusion | 200 g (from 200 g potatoes) | Source of carbohydrates, vitamins | | Dextrose (Glucose) | **20 g** | Fermentable carbon source | | Agar | **15–20 g** | Solidifying agent | | Distilled water | 1000 mL | Solvent | | **pH (natural)** | **5.6 ± 0.2** | Slightly acidic | | **pH (acidified)** | **3.5 ± 0.1** | After tartaric acid addition | ### The Acidification Trick — Why pH 3.5? Bacteria thrive at **neutral pH (6.5–7.5)**. By dropping the pH to **3.5**, we create an environment where: - **Bacteria** (especially Gram-positive spoilage types) are **inhibited** - **Yeasts and moulds** (acid-tolerant fungi) can still **grow vigorously** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why add acid AFTER autoclaving?** Autoclaving acidified media causes **caramelization of dextrose** (browning) and degrades the agar. The standard protocol is to **autoclave PDA at pH 5.6, cool to 45–50 °C, then aseptically add sterile 10% tartaric acid** to lower pH to 3.5. ### Alternative Antifungal-Bacterial Supplements - **Chloramphenicol (50 mg/L)** — broad-spectrum bacterial inhibitor (replaces acidification in some protocols) - **Rose Bengal (50 mg/L)** + Chlortetracycline — for **yeasts and moulds in dairy** --- ## 🧬 2.3 Colony Morphology — Reading the Plate A KTU examiner expects students to **differentiate yeast colonies from mould colonies** by macroscopic and microscopic features. ### 🟡 Yeast Colony Characteristics | Feature | Description | |---------|-------------| | **Colour** | Cream, white, pink, or off-white | | **Texture** | Buttery, smooth, glossy, **mucoid** | | **Elevation** | Convex, raised | | **Margin** | Entire (smooth-edged) | | **Size** | 1–3 mm diameter | | **Microscopy** | Single, oval, budding cells (5–10 μm) | | **Examples** | *Saccharomyces*, *Candida*, *Rhodotorula* | ### 🟢 Mould Colony Characteristics | Feature | Description | |---------|-------------| | **Colour** | Green, black, blue-green, white, cottony, velvety | | **Texture** | Fluffy, cottony, powdery, **filamentous** | | **Elevation** | Flat with aerial hyphae (often "fuzzy" halo) | | **Margin** | Irregular, diffuse, spreading | | **Size** | 2–10 cm (rapidly expanding) | | **Microscopy** | Long, branching, septate hyphae with conidiophores | | **Examples** | *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Fusarium*, *Mucor*, *Rhizopus* | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick Trick:** "Yeasts are **shiny and small**; Moulds are **fuzzy and big**." --- ## 📊 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | **CFU/g = (N × D) / V** | Colony forming units per gram | CFU/g | N = colony count, D = dilution factor, V = volume plated | | 2 | **CFU/mL = (N × D) / V** | Colony forming units per mL | CFU/mL | For liquid samples | | 3 | **D = 1/(Dilution)** | Inverse of dilution | Dimensionless | e.g., 10⁻⁴ → D = 10⁴ | | 4 | **Dilution = (Sample Volume) / (Total Volume)** | Fold-dilution | Dimensionless | e.g., 1 mL in 9 mL → 1/10 | | 5 | **CFU/g = (Σ N_i) / (Σ V_i × D_i)** | Weighted average for duplicate plates | CFU/g | For multiple plates, use mean of countable plates | | 6 | **Log₁₀(CFU/g) = log₁₀(N × D / V)** | Logarithmic representation | log CFU/g | Used in shelf-life studies | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** Only plates with **30 ≤ N ≤ 300** colonies are statistically valid. Plates outside this range must be marked as **TNTC** (Too Numerous To Count) or **TFTC** (Too Few To Count). You report the count from the **single dilution** falling in the countable range, OR take a **weighted mean** if two consecutive dilutions are valid. ### Worked Numerical Example (Pre-Section 3 Setup) Suppose you plate 1.0 mL of the **10⁻⁴ dilution** of a fruit juice sample and after 3 days of incubation at 25 °C, you count **145 colonies** on the pour plate. $$ \text{CFU/mL} = \frac{145 \times 10^4}{1.0} = 1.45 \times 10^6 \text{ CFU/mL} $$ # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 📝 3.1 Full Derivation of the Standard Plate Count (SPC) Formula ### 🧾 Problem Statement *"25 g of a bread sample is homogenized in 225 mL of sterile diluent. Serial 10-fold dilutions are prepared. From the 10⁻⁵ dilution, 1.0 mL is plated in duplicate by pour plate method. After 5 days at 25 °C, plate A shows 142 colonies and plate B shows 158 colonies. Calculate the yeast and mould count in CFU/g."* --- ### 🔍 Step 1 — Identify the Initial Sample Suspension The first dilution is always: $$ D_1 = \frac{25 \text{ g sample}}{25 \text{ g} + 225 \text{ mL diluent}} $$ > **Conversion logic:** The 25 g of food sample displaces negligible volume, so the **total volume ≈ 250 mL**, but the **mass used is 25 g**. Therefore, the **first dilution is 1/10 = 10⁻¹**. $$ D_1 = \frac{1}{10} = 10^{-1} $$ --- ### 🔍 Step 2 — Identify the Plated Dilution The 10⁻⁵ dilution is the one plated. So the **plated dilution factor** is: $$ D_{\text{plated}} = 10^{-5} $$ In **inverse notation** (as used in the standard formula): $$ D = \frac{1}{10^{-5}} = 10^5 $$ --- ### 🔍 Step 3 — Compute the Mean Colony Count For duplicate plates: $$ N_{\text{mean}} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{2} = \frac{142 + 158}{2} $$ $$ N_{\text{mean}} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ colonies} $$ --- ### 🔍 Step 4 — Verify Statistical Validity (30–300 Rule) $$ 30 \leq 150 \leq 300 \quad \checkmark $$ > **Valid plate count confirmed.** --- ### 🔍 Step 5 — Apply the Master Formula The standard KTU formula for **CFU per gram** (for a 25 g sample in 225 mL diluent) is: $$ \text{CFU/g} = \frac{N_{\text{mean}} \times D_{\text{plated inverse}}}{V_{\text{plated}}} $$ Substituting values: $$ \text{CFU/g} = \frac{150 \times 10^5}{1.0 \text{ mL}} $$ $$ \text{CFU/g} = 1.50 \times 10^7 \text{ CFU/g} $$ --- ### 🔍 Step 6 — Logarithmic Conversion (Optional, Often Asked) $$ \log_{10}(\text{CFU/g}) = \log_{10}(1.50 \times 10^7) $$ $$ = \log_{10}(1.50) + 7 = 0.176 + 7 = 7.176 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CFU/g} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ (or } 7.18 \text{ log CFU/g)}} $$ --- ## 📊 3.2 The Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison | Parameter | Pour Plate (Acidified PDA) | Spread Plate (Acidified PDA) | Direct Microscopic Count (Hemocytometer) | Methylene Blue Staining (Yeast Viability) | |-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------| | **Volume Plated** | 1.0 mL | 0.1 mL | N/A (counted directly) | N/A (stained smear) | | **Result Type** | Viable count | Viable count | Total count (viable + dead) | Viable + non-viable differentiation | | **Selectivity** | High (pH 3.5) | High (pH 3.5) | None (no medium) | None (vital stain) | | **Incubation Time** | 3–5 days | 3–5 days | None (immediate) | None (immediate) | | **Heat Shock Risk** | Yes (molten agar 45 °C) | No | No | No | | **Identification** | Colony morphology only | Colony morphology + microscopy | Cell morphology only | Live vs. dead cells | | **Sensitivity** | 10² CFU/g | 10³ CFU/g | 10⁵ CFU/g | 10⁵ CFU/g | | **KTU Use Case** | Official APHA/IS standard | Pure culture enumeration | Rapid screening | Brewery QC | --- ## 🧫 3.3 Special Case — The Hemocytometer (Yeast Cell Counting) In brewing and wine industries, yeasts are also counted using a **Neubauer improved hemocytometer** under a microscope. ### Master Formula for Hemocytometer $$ \text{Cells/mL} = N_{\text{counted}} \times \text{Dilution Factor} \times 10^4 $$ > **Why 10⁴?** The standard Neubauer chamber has a depth of **0.1 mm** and a counting area of **1 mm²** for the central large square (1 mm × 1 mm × 0.1 mm = 0.1 mm³ = 10⁻⁴ mL). So 1 counted cell in that square = 10⁴ cells/mL. ### Worked Hemocytometer Example *"You count 75 yeast cells in the central large square of a Neubauer chamber. The yeast sample was diluted 1:100. Calculate the cell concentration."* $$ \text{Cells/mL} = 75 \times 100 \times 10^4 $$ $$ = 75 \times 10^6 = 7.5 \times 10^7 \text{ cells/mL} $$ --- ## 🧪 3.4 Serial Dilution Derivation — Mathematical Foundation Each transfer of 1 mL into 9 mL diluent yields: $$ \text{Dilution per step} = \frac{1 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ mL} + 9 \text{ mL}} = \frac{1}{10} $$ After **n** successive transfers: $$ D_n = \left(\frac{1}{10}\right)^n = 10^{-n} $$ ### Table of Cumulative Dilutions | Step | Transfer | Cumulative Dilution | Tube Label | |------|----------|---------------------|------------| | 0 | 25 g in 225 mL | 10⁻¹ | Stock | | 1 | 1 mL in 9 mL | 10⁻² | 2 | | 2 | 1 mL in 9 mL | 10⁻³ | 3 | | 3 | 1 mL in 9 mL | 10⁻⁴ | 4 | | 4 | 1 mL in 9 mL | 10⁻⁵ | 5 | | 5 | 1 mL in 9 mL | 10⁻⁶ | 6 | | 6 | 1 mL in 9 mL | 10⁻⁷ | 7 | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Hack:** "1 mL in 9 mL = 1/10 dilution" is the **universal rule**. If the dilution is "0.5 mL in 4.5 mL", it's also 1/10. The arithmetic is **(sample volume) / (total volume)**. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🧭 4.1 Complete Workflow — Yeast & Mould Enumeration ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Food Sample 25g or 25mL] --> B[Homogenize in 225 mL
0.1% Peptone Water] B --> C["First Dilution 10 power -1
Stock Suspension"] C --> D[Transfer 1 mL into 9 mL
Blank Tube] D --> E["10 power -2
Dilution Tube"] E --> F[Repeat 1 mL to 9 mL
transfers] F --> G["10 power -3 to 10 power -7
Serial Dilutions"] G --> H{Choose Plating
Method} H -->|Pour Plate| I[Pipette 1.0 mL
into empty plate] H -->|Spread Plate| J[Pour PDA plate
let solidify] I --> K[Add 15 to 20 mL
molten PDA 45 degrees] K --> L[Swirl 8 pattern
mix uniformly] L --> M[Let solidify
10 to 15 min] M --> N[Invert and Incubate
25 degrees 3 to 5 days] J --> O[Pipette 0.1 mL
on agar surface] O --> P[Spread with sterile
Drigalski rod] P --> Q[Absorb 10 to 15 min] Q --> N N --> R[Colony Counter
Count CFU] R --> S{30 to 300
Rule Valid?} S -->|Yes| T[Calculate CFU per g
or mL] S -->|No| U[Mark TNTC or TFTC
Use other dilution] T --> V[Report Result
CFU per g] ``` ## 🧬 4.2 Block Architecture — Identification & Differentiation Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Incubated Plate
3 to 5 days] --> B{Colony Type} B -->|Smooth Glossy
Cream White| C[Yeast Suspect] B -->|Fuzzy Cottony
Green Black| D[Mould Suspect] C --> E[Gram Stain
Microscopy] D --> F[Lactophenol Cotton Blue
Mount] E --> G[Oval Budding Cells
5 to 10 micrometer] F --> H[Septate Hyphae
with Conidiophores] G --> I[Confirm Yeast
Saccharomyces Candida] H --> J[Confirm Mould
Aspergillus Penicillium
Fusarium Mucor] I --> K[Subculture on
YPD Agar] J --> L[Subculture on
Czapek Dox Agar] ``` ## 🧪 4.3 Media Preparation Flow — Acidified PDA ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Weigh 39 g
Dehydrated PDA] --> B[Dissolve in 1000 mL
Distilled Water] B --> C[Adjust pH to 5.6] C --> D[Autoclave
121 degrees 15 psi
15 minutes] D --> E[Cool to 45 to 50 degrees
in water bath] E --> F[Aseptically add
10% Tartaric Acid
to pH 3.5] F --> G[Pour 15 to 20 mL
per sterile plate] G --> H[Plates Ready
for Use or Storage] ``` ## 📊 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Yeast Mould
Counting Method] --> B{Sample Type} B -->|Solid Food
Bread Cheese| C[Stomacher Blender
25 g in 225 mL] B -->|Liquid Food
Juice Wine| D[Direct Dilution
1 mL in 9 mL] C --> E[Heat Sensitive
Mould Spores?] D --> E E -->|Yes| F[Spread Plate
0.1 mL] E -->|No| G[Pour Plate
1.0 mL] F --> H[Read at 25 degrees
3 to 5 days] G --> H H --> I[Identify Yeast vs Mould
Colony Morphology] I --> J[Calculate CFU per g
or mL] ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] **Differentiate between pour plate and spread plate techniques for yeast and mould enumeration. State one advantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Pour Plate | Spread Plate | |---------|-----------|--------------| | Volume plated | 1.0 mL | 0.1 mL | | Sequence | Sample first, then agar | Agar first, then sample | | Mixing | Swirled with molten agar | Spread with L-rod | | **Advantage** | Standard APHA/IS official method; colonies grow in the agar matrix | No heat shock — ideal for sensitive moulds; surface colonies are easy to count and identify morphologically | --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] **Why is Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA) acidified to pH 3.5 before use in yeast and mould counting? What reagent is used for acidification?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** PDA is acidified to **pH 3.5** because most **bacteria cannot grow below pH 4.0**, while yeasts and moulds are **acid-tolerant** and grow well in this range. This makes the medium **selective for fungi** by suppressing bacterial overgrowth. **Reagent used:** Sterile **10% Tartaric acid** solution, added aseptically **after autoclaving** (when the medium has cooled to 45–50 °C) to prevent caramelization of dextrose. --- ## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the **standard pour plate method** for enumeration of yeasts and moulds in a fruit juice sample. Include details of medium, dilution, incubation, and result calculation. **(b)** [7 marks] A fruit juice sample (25 mL) is homogenized in 225 mL of diluent. Serial dilutions are prepared. From the 10⁻⁴ dilution, **1.0 mL** is plated by pour plate method in duplicate. After 4 days at 25 °C, **Plate 1 = 215 colonies**, **Plate 2 = 245 colonies**. From the 10⁻⁵ dilution, 1.0 mL gives **Plate 3 = 28 colonies**, **Plate 4 = 32 colonies**. Calculate the **yeast and mould count in CFU/mL**. --- ### **Model Answer for Question A** #### Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks) 1. **Sample Preparation (1 mark):** Mix 25 mL of fruit juice with 225 mL of sterile **0.1% peptone water** in a sterile Erlenmeyer flask. Shake vigorously to obtain the **10⁻¹ dilution**. 2. **Serial Dilution (1 mark):** Transfer 1 mL of 10⁻¹ into 9 mL of sterile diluent → 10⁻². Repeat stepwise up to **10⁻⁶**. 3. **Medium Preparation (1 mark):** Prepare **acidified Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA)**: dissolve 39 g of dehydrated PDA in 1000 mL distilled water, autoclave at **121 °C / 15 psi for 15 min**, cool to 45–50 °C, and aseptically add sterile **10% tartaric acid** to lower pH to **3.5 ± 0.1**. 4. **Inoculation (1 mark):** Pipette **1.0 mL** from selected dilutions (e.g., 10⁻³, 10⁻⁴, 10⁻⁵) into **empty, labelled sterile Petri plates** in duplicate. 5. **Pouring & Mixing (1 mark):** Pour **15–20 mL** of cooled molten PDA into each plate; swirl gently in an 8-shaped pattern. 6. **Incubation (1 mark):** Allow solidification, invert plates, and incubate at **25 ± 1 °C for 3–5 days**. 7. **Counting (1 mark):** Count colonies on plates showing **30–300 CFU** using a colony counter. Identify yeasts (smooth, creamy) vs moulds (fuzzy, filamentous) by colony morphology. Calculate CFU/mL using the standard formula. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Verify each dilution's counts (1 mark):** - 10⁻⁴ dilution: Mean = (215 + 245)/2 = **230 colonies** → 30 ≤ 230 ≤ 300 ✓ **Valid** - 10⁻⁵ dilution: Mean = (28 + 32)/2 = **30 colonies** → 30 ≤ 30 ≤ 300 ✓ **Valid (boundary)** **Step 2 — Choose the standard (most reliable) dilution (1 mark):** The 10⁻⁴ dilution gives 230 colonies, well within the optimal range. We use this value. **Step 3 — Apply the formula (3 marks):** $$ \text{CFU/mL} = \frac{N_{\text{mean}} \times D_{\text{inverse}}}{V_{\text{plated}}} $$ $$ = \frac{230 \times 10^4}{1.0 \text{ mL}} $$ $$ = 2.3 \times 10^6 \text{ CFU/mL} $$ **Step 4 — Logarithmic Expression (2 marks):** $$ \log_{10}(2.3 \times 10^6) = \log_{10}(2.3) + 6 = 0.362 + 6 = 6.362 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CFU/mL} = 2.3 \times 10^6 \text{ or } 6.36 \log \text{ CFU/mL}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** [7 marks] What is **acidified PDA**? Explain its composition, principle of selectivity, and procedure for its preparation in the laboratory. **(b)** [7 marks] A bread sample (25 g) is mixed with 225 mL diluent. From the 10⁻³ dilution, **0.1 mL** is spread on acidified PDA plates. After incubation at 25 °C for 5 days, **Plate 1 = 152 colonies**, **Plate 2 = 168 colonies**. From the 10⁻⁴ dilution, 0.1 mL gives **Plate 3 = 18 colonies**, **Plate 4 = 22 colonies**. Calculate **yeast and mould count in CFU/g**. --- ### **Model Answer for Question B** #### Part (a) — Acidified PDA (7 marks) 1. **Definition (1 mark):** Acidified Potato Dextrose Agar is a selective mycological medium prepared by adjusting the pH of PDA to **3.5 ± 0.1** using sterile **10% tartaric acid** added after autoclaving. 2. **Composition (2 marks):** PDA contains potato infusion (from 200 g potatoes), dextrose (20 g/L), and agar (15–20 g/L). After autoclaving at pH 5.6, it is cooled and acidified. 3. **Principle of Selectivity (2 marks):** The low pH (**3.5**) suppresses the growth of **bacteria** (which prefer neutral pH), while **yeasts and moulds** tolerate acidic conditions. The high dextrose concentration favours rapid fungal growth. 4. **Preparation Procedure (2 marks):** - Suspend 39 g of dehydrated PDA in 1000 mL distilled water - Sterilize by autoclaving at **121 °C / 15 psi for 15 minutes** - Cool to **45–50 °C** in a water bath - Aseptically add **sterile 10% tartaric acid** to pH 3.5 - Pour 15–20 mL per sterile Petri plate --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Mean count of valid plates (2 marks):** - 10⁻³ dilution: Mean = (152 + 168)/2 = **160 colonies** → 30 ≤ 160 ≤ 300 ✓ **Valid** - 10⁻⁴ dilution: Mean = (18 + 22)/2 = **20 colonies** → Below 30 ✗ **TFTC, invalid** **Step 2 — Use the 10⁻³ dilution only (1 mark):** $$ N_{\text{mean}} = 160, \quad D = 10^3, \quad V = 0.1 \text{ mL} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the formula (2 marks):** $$ \text{CFU/g} = \frac{160 \times 10^3}{0.1 \text{ mL}} $$ $$ = \frac{160 \times 10^3}{10^{-1}} = 160 \times 10^4 = 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ CFU/g} $$ **Step 4 — Logarithmic form (2 marks):** $$ \log_{10}(1.6 \times 10^6) = 0.204 + 6 = 6.204 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CFU/g} = 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ or } 6.20 \text{ log CFU/g}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the inverse of dilution factor** — using 10⁻⁴ directly instead of 10⁴. This is the #1 cause of wrong answers. > 2. **Mixing up volume plated** — 0.1 mL for spread plate, NOT 1.0 mL. Always read the question carefully. > 3. **Forgetting units** — Report as **CFU/g for solids, CFU/mL for liquids**. No units = lose 0.5 mark. > 4. **Using invalid plates (TFTC/TNTC)** — Never use plates with <30 or >300 colonies for final calculation. > 5. **Wrong acid** — Some students write "acetic acid" or "HCl". The correct reagent is **tartaric acid**. > 6. **Autoclaving at acidic pH** — The medium must be autoclaved at **pH 5.6** and acidified **afterwards**. This is a 2-mark distinction. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 KTU Last-Minute Revision Card** - ⭐ **Gold Standard Method:** Pour plate on **acidified PDA (pH 3.5)** with incubation at **25 °C for 3–5 days**. - ⭐ **Acidification Reagent:** Sterile **10% tartaric acid**, added **AFTER autoclaving** (to prevent dextrose caramelization). - ⭐ **Standard Sample:** **25 g (or 25 mL) in 225 mL** diluent = 10⁻¹ initial dilution. - ⭐ **Dilution Rule:** **1 mL in 9 mL = 1/10 dilution** (always). - ⭐ **Volume Plated:** **1.0 mL (pour plate)** | **0.1 mL (spread plate)**. - ⭐ **Countable Range:** **30 ≤ CFU ≤ 300 per plate** (always). - ⭐ **Master Formula:** **CFU/g (or mL) = (N × D) / V**, where D = 10ⁿ. - ⭐ **Yeast Morphology:** Smooth, creamy, glossy, oval budding cells (5–10 μm). - ⭐ **Mould Morphology:** Cottony, fuzzy, filamentous, septate hyphae with conidia. - ⭐ **Why not bacterial medium?** Bacteria grow at 37 °C; fungi are mesophilic at **25 °C**. - ⭐ **For Hemocytometer:** Total cells/mL = N × dilution × 10⁴. - ⭐ **Units:** **CFU/g** for solids (bread, cheese, meat) | **CFU/mL** for liquids (juice, milk, wine). - ⭐ **Heat-Sensitive Moulds → Spread Plate (no heat shock).** - ⭐ **Reference Standards:** **APHA, IS 5403 (for yeasts and moulds in foods).** - ⭐ **Selective Supplements:** **Rose Bengal (50 mg/L)** for dairy; **Chloramphenicol (50 mg/L)** for bacterial suppression in alternative protocols.

topics.title: Demonstration of Microbial Production of Curd
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧫 Demonstration of Microbial Production of Curd

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL307 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstration of Microbial Production of Curd

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Curd** is a fermented dairy product obtained by the **lactic acid fermentation** of milk, wherein specific **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)** — primarily *Lactobacillus bulgaricus*, *Streptococcus thermophilus*, and *Lactococcus lactis* — convert **lactose** into **lactic acid**, causing **casein precipitation** and the formation of a semi-solid coagulated mass with characteristic tangy flavor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microbial Production of Curd** is a classic demonstration of **controlled batch fermentation** in food microbiology, exhibiting the symbiotic relationship between two LAB strains and showcasing the principle of **probiotic food preparation**.

### 🔑 Key Microorganisms Involved

| Microorganism | Type | Optimal Temperature | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Lactobacillus bulgaricus* | Gram-positive rod | 42–45 °C | Produces acetaldehyde (flavor) + lactic acid |
| *Streptococcus thermophilus* | Gram-positive coccus | 37–42 °C | Produces lactic acid, reduces oxygen |
| *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* | Gram-positive coccus | 30 °C | Acidification, texture development |

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of curd-making as "microbial teamwork in a warm bath":**
> Imagine you have a glass of warm sweet milk. You add a tiny spoonful of yesterday's curd (the **starter culture** — like passing a baton in a relay race). Inside this warm cozy environment, billions of invisible **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)** wake up, eat the **milk sugar (lactose)**, and excrete **lactic acid** as waste. This acid:
> 1. **Sours the milk** (the tangy taste).
> 2. **Drops the pH** below the isoelectric point of **casein (pH ≈ 4.6)**, causing milk proteins to **clump together (coagulate)** — like how lemon curdles milk.
> 3. The once-liquid milk becomes a **semi-solid gel** — that is your **curd**!
>
> This is nature's most ancient form of **biotechnology** — used for over 5,000 years before humans even knew microbes existed.

### 📌 Standard Operational Parameters (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

- **Inoculum size:** 1–2% (v/v) of starter culture
- **Incubation temperature:** **37 °C** to **45 °C**
- **Incubation time:** **6 to 12 hours**
- **Final pH of curd:** **4.4 – 4.6**
- **Titrable acidity:** **0.7 – 1.0% lactic acid**
- **Isoelectric point of casein:** **pH 4.6**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 The Biochemistry of Curd Formation — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The entire process of curd formation can be broken down into **four sequential biochemical stages**. Understanding the *why* behind each step is critical for KTU viva voce and theory questions.

**Stage 1 — Lactose Hydrolysis (The Energy Step)**

Bacteria use the enzyme **β-galactosidase (lactase)** to split the disaccharide lactose into its two monosaccharide units:

$$\text{Lactose} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-galactosidase}} \text{Glucose} + \text{Galactose}$$

**Stage 2 — Glycolysis & Homolactic Fermentation**

Glucose and galactose enter the **Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) pathway**, ultimately producing **pyruvate**, which is then reduced to **lactic acid** by the enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase**:

$$\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{EMP pathway}} 2 \text{ Pyruvate} \xrightarrow{\text{LDH}} 2 \text{ Lactic Acid}$$

**Stage 3 — pH Drop & Casein Coagulation (The Magic Step)**

As lactic acid accumulates, the **pH drops progressively**. When pH reaches the **isoelectric point of casein (pH 4.6)**, the net charge on casein micelles becomes **zero**, causing them to aggregate via **calcium phosphate bridges** and form a **continuous protein network (gel)** that traps water, fat globules, and bacteria.

**Stage 4 — Symbiotic Relationship (Protocooperation)**

| Strain | Secretes | Benefit to Partner |
|---|---|---|
| *S. thermophilus* | Formic acid, CO₂ | Stimulates *L. bulgaricus* growth |
| *L. bulgaricus* | Peptides, amino acids (from casein hydrolysis) | Stimulates *S. thermophilus* growth |

This **mutualistic protocooperation** is what makes the combination so efficient.

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear regularly in KTU university exams.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Titrable Acidity** | $\% \text{ Acidity} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.009 \times 100}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | % lactic acid | Quality check of curd |
| 2 | **pH vs. Acidity Relation** | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | dimensionless | Monitor fermentation |
| 3 | **Lactose Conversion** | $\text{Lactose} \rightarrow \text{Glucose} + \text{Galactose} \rightarrow 4 \text{ Lactic Acid}$ | g/L | Theoretical yield |
| 4 | **Isoelectric Coagulation** | Casein coagulates at $\text{pH} \approx 4.6$ | pH | Gel formation trigger |
| 5 | **Optimum Temperature (LAB)** | $T_{\text{opt}} = 37\text{–}45\,°\text{C}$ | °C | Incubation setting |
| 6 | **Generation Time of LAB** | $t_d = 30\text{–}45 \text{ min}$ at 37 °C | minutes | Growth kinetics |
| 7 | **Heat Treatment Constant** | $F = 72\,°\text{C for } 15 \text{ s (HTST pasteurization)}$ | °C·s | Pre-fermentation step |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the `|` symbol inside the table** — always use `\vert` if absolute value is needed. KTU digital evaluation systems can misrender vertical pipes.

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Dairy Plants**: Design of **batch fermenters** for dahi, yogurt, and cheese production.
- **Probiotic Industries**: Selection of **thermo-tolerant LAB strains** for shelf-stable fermented foods.
- **Food Safety Labs**: pH and titrable acidity are **regulatory parameters** under **FSSAI (India)** and **Codex Alimentarius**.
- **Biotechnology**: Starter culture production, lyophilization, and **direct-to-vat (DVS) culture technology**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The following is the standard KTU PCFTL307 lab protocol. Each step is exhaustive — KTU examiners value procedural accuracy above all else.**

### 🧪 A. Materials and Equipment Required

| Category | Items |
|---|---|
| **Glassware** | Conical flasks (250 mL × 4), beakers, measuring cylinder, pipettes (1 mL, 10 mL), burette |
| **Reagents** | Fresh cow's/buffalo's milk (full fat), commercial curd (starter), phenolphthalein indicator, **0.1 N NaOH** solution, distilled water |
| **Instruments** | pH meter, thermometer, hot plate, autoclave, incubator, analytical balance |
| **Miscellaneous** | Cotton plugs, aluminum foil, spatula, muslin cloth, labeling tape |

### 📋 B. Step-by-Step Procedure

#### **Part I — Preparation of Milk Substrate**

**Step 1:** Measure **200 mL of fresh full-fat milk** into a clean 250 mL conical flask.

**Step 2:** Heat the milk to **boiling (100 °C)** for **5 minutes**.
- **Why?** This achieves **pasteurization** — kills competing vegetative pathogens (*E. coli*, *Salmonella*) and **denatures whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin), improving curd texture.

**Step 3:** Cool the milk rapidly to **42–45 °C** (use a sterile thermometer).
- **Why?** This is the **optimum growth temperature** for *L. bulgaricus* and *S. thermophilus*. Higher temperatures kill the starter; lower temperatures slow fermentation.

#### **Part II — Inoculation with Starter Culture**

**Step 4:** Aseptically add **2–5 g (≈ 1–2% v/v) of fresh commercial curd** as the **starter culture (inoculum)** to the cooled milk.
- **Why?** Commercial curd contains live LAB in logarithmic/decline phase — they act as the **microbial seed**.

**Step 5:** Mix gently with a sterile glass rod using **circular stirring** (≈ 20 rotations) — avoid foaming.
- **Why?** Homogeneous distribution of inoculum ensures **uniform fermentation**.

#### **Part III — Incubation**

**Step 6:** Cover the flask mouth with **cotton plug + aluminum foil** and label the flask with:
- Name, Date, Sample ID, Inoculum %, Temperature.

**Step 7:** Place the flask in a **BOD incubator at 42 °C** for **8–12 hours**.
- **Why BOD incubator?** Provides **isothermal conditions** (±0.5 °C) essential for reproducible microbial growth.

#### **Part IV — Observation and Analysis**

**Step 8:** After incubation, observe and record:
- **Texture:** Smooth semi-solid set ✓
- **Color:** Creamy white
- **Odor:** Pleasant, tangy-sour
- **Whey separation (syneresis):** Minimal in good curd

**Step 9:** Measure **pH** using a **calibrated digital pH meter**.

**Step 10:** Determine **Titrable Acidity** by titrating against **0.1 N NaOH** using **phenolphthalein indicator**.

### 📐 C. Analytical Calculation — Titrable Acidity (Worked Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This calculation is a KTU board favorite — practice it thoroughly.**

**Given:**
- Volume of curd sample ($V_{\text{sample}}$) = **10 mL**
- Normality of NaOH ($N_{\text{NaOH}}$) = **0.1 N**
- Burette reading (titre value) ($V_{\text{NaOH}}$) = **8.5 mL**
- Equivalent weight of lactic acid = **0.009** (since 1 mL of 1 N NaOH = 0.009 g lactic acid)

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Titrable Acidity (as lactic acid)} &= \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.009 \times 100}{V_{\text{sample}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.009 \times 100}{10} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.765}{10} \\[6pt]
&= 0.765\% \text{ lactic acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The acidity of **0.765%** falls within the standard range of **0.7–1.0%**, indicating a **well-fermented, good quality curd**. ✅

### 🔬 D. Microbial Confirmation Test (Optional Advanced Step)

A **Gram stain** of a curd smear shows:
- **Purple rods** (*Lactobacillus* — Gram-positive)
- **Purple cocci in chains** (*Streptococcus* — Gram-positive)
- **No Gram-negative organisms** (confirms culture purity)

### 📊 E. Observation Recording Table (For KTU Record Submission)

| Parameter | Observation | Standard Value | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial pH of milk | 6.6 | 6.5–6.8 | Normal fresh milk |
| Initial temperature | 42 °C | 37–45 °C | Optimal |
| Final pH of curd | 4.5 | 4.4–4.6 | Acceptable |
| Titrable acidity | 0.77% | 0.7–1.0% | Good quality |
| Texture | Semi-solid, smooth | Set, no syneresis | Properly coagulated |
| Aroma | Mild sour, pleasant | Tangy | Flavor compounds formed |
| Color | Creamy white | White/off-white | Normal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔄 A. Process Flowchart — Curd Production Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fresh Raw Milk] --> B[Boiling at 100 degC for 5 min]
    B --> C[Pasteurized Milk - Sterile]
    C --> D[Cool to 42-45 degC]
    D --> E[Add 1-2 percent Starter Culture]
    E --> F[Homogeneous Mixing]
    F --> G[Incubate at 42 degC for 8-12 hrs]
    G --> H{Lactic Acid reaches pH 4.6}
    H -- Yes --> I[Casein Coagulation at Isoelectric Point]
    I --> J[Semi-solid Curd Gel Forms]
    J --> K[Refrigeration at 4 degC]
    K --> L[Final Product: Curd Ready]
    H -- No --> G
    L --> M[Quality Tests: pH, Acidity, Texture]
    M --> N[End: Packaged Curd]
```

### 🧬 B. Biochemical Pathway — Lactose to Lactic Acid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lactose - Milk Sugar] -->|Beta-galactosidase| B[Glucose + Galactose]
    B -->|EMP Pathway| C[Pyruvate]
    C -->|Lactate Dehydrogenase| D[Lactic Acid]
    D -->|pH drops below 4.6| E[Casein Coagulation]
    E --> F[Curd Gel Formation]
```

### 🏗️ C. Block Diagram — Lab-Scale Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Sample Preparation Block"]
        P1[Milk 200 mL] --> P2[Boiling - Pasteurization]
        P2 --> P3[Cooling to 42 degC]
    end
    subgraph S2["Inoculation Block"]
        P3 --> P4[Add Starter Culture 1-2 percent]
        P4 --> P5[Sterile Mixing]
    end
    subgraph S3["Fermentation Block"]
        P5 --> P6[BOD Incubator 42 degC]
        P6 --> P7[8-12 Hours Incubation]
    end
    subgraph S4["Analysis Block"]
        P7 --> P8[pH Meter Reading]
        P7 --> P9[Titration with 0.1 N NaOH]
        P7 --> P10[Organoleptic Evaluation]
        P8 --> P11[Report]
        P9 --> P11
        P10 --> P11
    end
```

### 🤝 D. Symbiotic Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SymbioticPair["LAB Protocooperation in Curd"]
        direction LR
        S1[Streptococcus thermophilus]
        L1[Lactobacillus bulgaricus]
    end
    S1 -->|Secretes Formic Acid and CO2| L1
    L1 -->|Releases Peptides and Amino Acids| S1
    S1 -->|Produces Lactic Acid| Product[Acidic Environment pH 4.6]
    L1 -->|Produces Lactic Acid and Acetaldehyde| Product
    Product --> Curd[Set Curd with Flavor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, Set A]**

**Q1. Define lactic acid fermentation. Name any two microorganisms involved in curd preparation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Lactic acid fermentation** is an anaerobic metabolic process in which carbohydrates (sugars) are converted primarily into **lactic acid** by the action of **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)**, without the involvement of an electron transport chain.
>
> The chemical equation is:
>
> $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{LAB}} 2 \text{ CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + 2 \text{ ATP}$$
>
> **Two microorganisms** involved in curd preparation are:
> 1. ***Lactobacillus bulgaricus*** — a Gram-positive, rod-shaped bacterium.
> 2. ***Streptococcus thermophilus*** — a Gram-positive, cocci-shaped bacterium.
>
> These two organisms exhibit a **symbiotic (protocooperative) relationship** that accelerates lactic acid production.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Set B]**

**Q2. What is the isoelectric point of casein, and why is it important in curd formation?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **isoelectric point (pI) of casein** is **pH 4.6**. At this pH, the **net electrical charge on casein micelles becomes zero**, eliminating the electrostatic repulsion that normally keeps them dispersed in milk.
>
> **Importance in curd formation:**
> 1. As LAB ferment lactose into lactic acid, the pH of milk progressively falls.
> 2. When pH reaches **4.6**, casein micelles **aggregate and coagulate**, forming a **three-dimensional protein network** that traps water, fat, and bacteria — this network is the **curd gel**.
> 3. Thus, the pI acts as the **critical trigger** that transforms liquid milk into semi-solid curd.

---

### 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2]**

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choose this OR Question B

**(a)** Describe in detail the **microbial production of curd** with reference to the **biochemistry, key microorganisms, and role of symbiosis**. Add a note on the **optimal conditions** required. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **procedure for determining titrable acidity of curd**. Include the **formula, working, and interpretation of results**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) [7 marks]**

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**
Curd is a **fermented dairy product** produced by the **lactic acid fermentation** of milk using specific Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB). It is one of the oldest biotechnological processes known to humankind.

**2. Key Microorganisms (2 marks)**
- ***Lactobacillus bulgaricus***: Gram-positive, non-spore-forming rod; grows best at **42–45 °C**; produces lactic acid and **acetaldehyde** (responsible for characteristic flavor).
- ***Streptococcus thermophilus***: Gram-positive coccus; grows at **37–42 °C**; rapidly produces lactic acid and reduces oxygen tension.
- ***Lactococcus lactis***: Used in mesophilic fermentations at **30 °C**.

**3. Biochemistry of Lactic Acid Production (2 marks)**
The overall reaction sequence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lactose} &\xrightarrow{\beta\text{-galactosidase}} \text{Glucose} + \text{Galactose} \\[4pt]
\text{Glucose} &\xrightarrow{\text{EMP pathway}} 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ ATP} \\[4pt]
\text{Pyruvate} &\xrightarrow{\text{Lactate dehydrogenase}} \text{Lactic Acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

The accumulated lactic acid lowers the pH to **4.6**, the **isoelectric point of casein**, triggering **coagulation** and gel formation.

**4. Symbiotic Relationship (1 mark)**
- *S. thermophilus* produces **formic acid and CO₂** that stimulate *L. bulgaricus*.
- *L. bulgaricus* hydrolyzes casein into **peptides and amino acids** that feed *S. thermophilus*.
- This **protocooperation** speeds up acid production.

**5. Optimal Conditions (1 mark)**
- Temperature: **37–45 °C**
- Inoculum: **1–2% (v/v)**
- Time: **8–12 hours**
- Final pH: **4.4–4.6**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) [7 marks]**

**1. Principle (1 mark)**
The acidity of curd is determined by **titration against standardized 0.1 N NaOH** using **phenolphthalein** as an indicator. The endpoint is reached when the solution turns a **faint pink color** (pH ≈ 8.2).

**2. Reagents Required (1 mark)**
- **0.1 N NaOH** (standardized)
- **Phenolphthalein indicator** (1% in ethanol)
- Distilled water
- Curd sample

**3. Procedure (2 marks)**
1. Weigh **10 g (or pipette 10 mL) of well-mixed curd** into a 100 mL conical flask.
2. Add **20 mL of distilled water** and **2–3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator**.
3. Titrate against **0.1 N NaOH** from the burette with **constant swirling** until a **persistent faint pink color** appears.
4. Note the **burette reading** ($V_{\text{NaOH}}$).
5. Repeat in **triplicate** and take the **mean value**.

**4. Calculation (2 marks)**
Using the standard formula:

$$\% \text{ Titrable Acidity} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.009 \times 100}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

The factor **0.009** is the equivalent weight of lactic acid in grams (since 1 mL of 1 N NaOH = 0.009 g of lactic acid, $M_w = 90$, equivalent weight = $90/1 = 90 \rightarrow 0.090$ per equivalent per 10 mL $\rightarrow$ 0.009 per mL of 1N per mL of sample basis is the FSSAI standard).

**5. Interpretation (1 mark)**
- **0.7–0.9% lactic acid** → Good quality curd
- **< 0.5%** → Under-fermented (sweet, no set)
- **> 1.2%** → Over-fermented (excessively sour, high syneresis)

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **role of pH, temperature, and inoculum size** in optimizing curd fermentation. What happens if these parameters are deviated from the optimum? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the **types of lactic acid fermentation** (homofermentative vs. heterofermentative) with examples of organisms in each category. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Optimum | Effect of Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **pH** | 4.4–4.6 (final) | Above → no coagulation; Below → harsh acidic curd |
| **Temperature** | 37–45 °C | Below → slow fermentation (>24 h); Above → starter death |
| **Inoculum** | 1–2% v/v | Less → slow acid production; More → very rapid, sour curd |
| **Time** | 8–12 h | Less → soft set; More → syneresis (whey separation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **temperature for *S. thermophilus* (37–42 °C)** with that of *L. bulgaricus* (42–45 °C). Always mention **42 °C** as the **compromise optimum** used industrially.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) [7 marks]**

| Feature | Homofermentative | Heterofermentative |
|---|---|---|
| **End products** | ≥ 85% lactic acid only | Lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol/acetic acid |
| **Pathway** | EMP pathway | Phosphoketolase pathway |
| **ATP yield** | 2 ATP/glucose | 1 ATP/glucose |
| **Examples** | *Lactobacillus bulgaricus*, *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Lactococcus lactis* | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *Lactobacillus fermentum*, *Oenococcus oeni* |
| **Application** | Curd, yogurt | Sauerkraut, sourdough, wine |

The heterofermentative pathway:

$$\text{Glucose} \rightarrow \text{Lactic Acid} + \text{Ethanol} + \text{CO}_2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always use the term "lactic acid bacteria (LAB)"** — not just "bacteria." KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for generic terminology.
> 2. **Always specify the isoelectric point as pH 4.6** — not just "low pH." The pI value is **mandatory** in KTU answers.
> 3. **Symbiosis = Protocooperation** — these terms are interchangeable but explicitly mentioning both earns bonus marks.
> 4. **Units matter!** Always express titrable acidity as **% lactic acid**, not just "percentage."
> 5. **In calculations, do not forget the factor 100** in the numerator — a common 1-mark deduction error.
> 6. **Do not confuse "starter culture" with "mother culture"** — starter is the working inoculum; mother culture is the preserved stock.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams**

- ✅ **Curd** = Lactic acid fermented dairy product
- ✅ **Primary LAB**: *L. bulgaricus* + *S. thermophilus* (symbiotic)
- ✅ **Biochemistry**: Lactose → Glucose + Galactose → Pyruvate → Lactic Acid
- ✅ **Critical pH**: **4.6** (Isoelectric point of casein)
- ✅ **Optimal temperature**: **37–45 °C** (use **42 °C** in labs)
- ✅ **Inoculum size**: **1–2% v/v**
- ✅ **Incubation time**: **8–12 hours**
- ✅ **Titrable acidity standard**: **0.7–1.0% lactic acid**
- ✅ **Titrable acidity formula**: $(V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.009 \times 100) / V_{\text{sample}}$
- ✅ **Indicator**: Phenolphthalein (endpoint: faint pink)
- ✅ **Pasteurization**: **100 °C for 5 min** (lab) / **72 °C for 15 s** (industrial HTST)
- ✅ **Final curd pH**: **4.4–4.6**
- ✅ **Homofermentative** = ≥ 85% lactic acid only (EMP pathway)
- ✅ **Heterofermentative** = lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol (Phosphoketolase pathway)
- ✅ **Symbiosis type**: Protocooperation (mutualism without dependency)
- ✅ **Gram reaction of LAB**: **Gram-positive**
- ✅ **Quality check**: Smooth set, no syneresis, pleasant tang, pH 4.4–4.6, acidity 0.7–1.0%

> **🎓 Final Exam Tip:** Always present your answer in **structured sub-headings** (Principle → Procedure → Calculation → Inference). KTU examiners reward **clarity and organization** with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
